Mandatory online-only from 1 January 2025; accessible at mytax.hasil.gov.my under e-SPC module
Income Tax Act 1967, Section 83(3): employer must withhold final salary until SPC received
CP21 for employees leaving Malaysia for >3 months; CP22A for private sector; CP22B for government/statutory employer
Processing time: 14 working days — the 35-day alert provides buffer for processing before cessation date
Distinct from the existing CP22 (new hire) story — this covers the departure/cessation side

userStories.title: Register PCB Administrator Role on MyTax e-PCB Plus and Store Credential Reference on Company
userStories.description: As of 3 February 2025, all PCB monthly data submission and payments flow exclusively through the e-PCB Plus system on the MyTax portal. Before payroll staff can submit, the employer must designate a PCB Administrator (a specific named role on MyTax tied to an individual's NRIC) who is authorized to manage PCB submissions on behalf of the company. Without this setup, the e-PCB Plus file upload fails. Currently no story covers storing the PCB Administrator identity, managing Employer Representative delegation, or guiding the initial e-PCB Plus company registration.
acceptanceCriteria: Company DocType has a 'LHDN e-PCB Plus Settings' section with fields: 'PCB Administrator NRIC', 'PCB Administrator Name', 'Employer Representative NRIC' (optional), 'Employer Reference Number (E Number)', 'e-PCB Plus Registration Status' (Select: Not Registered / Registered)
LHDN Payroll workspace shows a setup checklist item: 'e-PCB Plus PCB Administrator registered' with status indicator
A dedicated 'e-PCB Plus Setup Guide' page in the LHDN Developer Tools panel links to the MyTax portal and documents the 4-step admin registration process
On CP39 file generation, system validates that 'e-PCB Plus Registration Status' = Registered before allowing download — if not, shows a blocking setup warning with link to the setup guide
Test: CP39 file generation on unregistered company raises validation error; registered company proceeds normally
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus mandatory from 3 February 2025: https://www.signature-trust.com/post/hasil-launches-integrated-e-pcb-plus-platform-on-mytax-portal
PCB Administrator setup guide: https://taxpod.com.my/articles/set-up-pcb-admin-mytax-epcb/
Employer reference number (E number) is issued by LHDN during employer registration and is required on all e-PCB Plus submissions
Role hierarchy: Employer (company director) > Employer Representative > PCB Administrator — each registered separately in MyTax with own NRIC
No direct API token for e-PCB Plus — all submission is via portal file upload (CSV/text format); this story is about the credential reference tracking, not automated API integration

userStories.title: Implement EPF i-Topup (Voluntary Excess Contribution) Payroll Deduction and RM7,000 PCB Relief Tracking
userStories.description: EPF's i-Topup facility allows employees to contribute more than the statutory EPF rate. The additional voluntary contribution (above mandatory 11%/9%) combines with compulsory EPF to form a combined RM7,000 PCB relief pool (RM4,000 for mandatory + RM3,000 for voluntary, shared with life insurance). Employers must submit i-Topup applications via i-Akaun (Employer) and may deduct the voluntary amount from the employee's salary. No existing story models voluntary EPF deductions distinct from mandatory, or tracks the combined RM7,000 relief cap for PCB.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Component 'EPF Voluntary (i-Topup)' of type Deduction exists in fixture, marked as non-statutory and voluntary
Employee record has 'EPF i-Topup Monthly Amount' (Currency) field to store the agreed voluntary top-up amount
Monthly payroll run deducts 'EPF Voluntary (i-Topup)' and adds to EPF contribution total for the month
YTD voluntary EPF deduction counter tracked per employee per year; combined with YTD mandatory EPF for relief cap validation
PCB calculator respects the RM7,000 combined relief limit: mandatory EPF relief capped at RM4,000; remaining RM3,000 shared with life insurance premium and additional voluntary EPF
TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType has 'Additional Voluntary EPF Contribution (i-Topup)' field auto-populated from YTD voluntary EPF deduction, subject to the RM3,000 sub-cap
EA Form reflects mandatory EPF and voluntary EPF contributions in their respective relief rows
technicalNotes: KWSP i-Topup Employer Guide: https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/employer/responsibilities/contributing-more-than-the-statutory-rate
KWSP i-Topup Member Guide: https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/savings/voluntary-excess
Tax relief structure: EPF mandatory RM4,000 (Section C, Income Tax Return); EPF voluntary + life insurance combined RM3,000 additional (same relief row); total EPF+life = RM7,000 max
Employer must submit KWSP Form 17A (Khas) via i-Akaun (Employer) to register employee's i-Topup amount before deducting from salary
Voluntary EPF is excluded from SOCSO, EIS, and HRDF contribution base calculations
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: Apply YA2025 PCB Schedule 1 Tax Brackets Including New 30% Tier for Income Exceeding RM2 Million
userStories.description: Malaysia's income tax Schedule 1 (Jadual Pertama) is updated for each year of assessment. For YA2025, the progressive rate structure is confirmed to include a top tier of 30% applicable to chargeable income exceeding RM2,000,000. The existing PCB calculator story (US-004) implements the base engine; this story ensures the YA2025 tax bracket table is correctly encoded and the LHDN PCB Specification 2025 amendments (relating to deduction item changes published in the official PCB 2025 PDF) are applied. The PCB spec is version-specific and must be maintained per assessment year.
acceptanceCriteria: Tax bracket table is stored in a configuration DocType or fixture keyed by assessment year, not hardcoded
PCB calculator selects the correct bracket table based on the payroll year (YA = payroll_year for Jan-Dec)
Unit tests verify correct tax for income of RM5,000, RM35,000, RM100,000, RM400,000, RM2,000,000, and RM2,500,000 against LHDN YA2025 PCB specification values
YA2024 calculations (for any historical recomputation) continue to use YA2024 brackets without interference
Personal tax rebate of RM400 applied when chargeable income does not exceed RM35,000 (confirmed unchanged for YA2025)
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB Specification 2025 PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
LHDN official tax rate page: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/individual/individual-life-cycle/income-declaration/tax-rate/
Budget 2025 confirmation: no change to MTD formula structure, but deduction item values revised for YA2025
30% top tier on chargeable income >RM2M is effective for YA2024 onwards (introduced Budget 2023); ensure YA2025 spec aligns
Store as 'PCB Tax Bracket' table with fields: assessment_year, chargeable_income_from, chargeable_income_to, rate_pct, cumulative_tax_at_lower_bound
dependencies: Implement PCB/MTD monthly calculation validation (US-004)

userStories.title: Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator (Section 60D Employment Act) with Replacement Leave Tracking
userStories.description: Employment Act 1955 Section 60D entitles EA-covered employees to 11 paid public holidays per year. When required to work on a public holiday, the employer must pay the employee their ordinary daily wage plus two additional days' wages (effectively triple pay), or alternatively offer an Off-In-Lieu replacement day. Overtime on a public holiday (beyond normal working hours) is at three times the hourly rate. For employees earning above RM4,000/month (not EA-covered), public holiday pay is governed by their employment contract. No existing payroll story models the Section 60D three-tier pay structure or replacement leave.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Slip can include a 'Public Holiday Work' additional earnings line that auto-computes: 2 x ORP (ordinary rate of pay per day) as the additional premium (the base day pay is already in the regular salary)
ORP per day for monthly-rated employees computed as monthly_salary / 26 per Section 60I of the Employment Act
Overtime on public holiday (hours beyond normal working hours) calculated at 3x hourly rate (3 x monthly_salary / 26 / normal_daily_hours)
Employee record tracks 'Public Holiday Off-In-Lieu Balance' counter; HR can convert public holiday worked into an OIL day credit instead of triple-pay
System validates that EA coverage applies (employee monthly wage <= RM4,000) before auto-applying EA rates; employees above threshold get a reminder to refer to employment contract
Public Holiday calendar integration: system flags payroll runs that include dates matching Malaysia's gazette public holidays
Tests: monthly RM3,000 employee works 8 hours on a PH → additional premium of RM3,000/26 x 2 = RM230.77; overtime beyond 8 hours → RM3,000/26/8 x 3 per OT hour
technicalNotes: Employment Act 1955, Section 60D: public holiday entitlement and payment obligations
Employment Act 1955, Section 60I: Ordinary Rate of Pay definition — monthly salary / 26 for monthly-rated employees
Employment Act 1955, Section 60A: overtime on public holiday at 3x hourly rate
Post-2022 amendment: EA covers all employees with monthly wages not exceeding RM4,000 for overtime/rest day purposes
Public holidays list maintained by MOHR gazette; state-specific public holidays also apply (state holidays differ by state)
dependencies: Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

userStories.title: Track Statutory Compliance Submission Calendar and Auto-Escalate Overdue Filings
userStories.description: Malaysian employers face multiple concurrent statutory filing deadlines each month: PCB (15th), EPF (15th), SOCSO (15th), EIS (15th), HRDF levy (15th), and various annual deadlines (EA Form by 28 Feb, Borang E by 31 Mar, CP22 within 30 days of hire, CP21/CP22A at least 30 days before cessation). Late filings attract automatic penalties — 10% late payment surcharge for EPF/PCB, 6% p.a. for SOCSO, RM200-RM20,000 fines for Borang E and EA Form. No existing story provides a unified compliance calendar that tracks whether all monthly submissions have been completed and escalates overdue items.
acceptanceCriteria: New DocType 'Statutory Compliance Submission' records: compliance type (PCB / EPF / SOCSO / EIS / HRDF / EA Form / Borang E / CP22 / CP21), payroll period/month, due date, submission status (Pending / Submitted / Overdue), submission date, reference number
Payroll Entry submission automatically creates Statutory Compliance Submission records for PCB, EPF, SOCSO, EIS, and HRDF for that month with due_date = last_day_of_month + 15 days
Dashboard widget 'Compliance Status' shows all pending submissions color-coded: green (>5 days remaining), amber (2-5 days), red (0-2 days or overdue)
Daily scheduled job sends email and Frappe notification to HR Manager for any submission reaching 0 days remaining without Submitted status
Annual filings (EA Form, Borang E) auto-created at year-end payroll run with their respective statutory deadlines
Penalty information tooltips show on overdue items: e.g., 'EPF late payment: 6% p.a. from due date'
technicalNotes: EPF: contributions due by 15th of following month — EPF Act 1991, Section 43
SOCSO: contributions due by 15th — ESSA 1969; 6% per annum late penalty
PCB: remittance due by 15th — ITA 1967, Section 83(6); 10% late payment penalty
EA Form: issued to employees by 28 February — ITA 1967, Section 83(1A)
Borang E: submitted to LHDN by 31 March — ITA 1967, Section 83(1)
HRDF levy: due by 15th of following month — PSMB Act 2001

userStories.title: Implement Approved Gratuity Fund Contribution and Tax-Exempt Gratuity Declaration for EA Form
userStories.description: Income Tax Act 1967, Schedule 6, paragraph 25 exempts gratuity paid from an LHDN-approved gratuity/pension fund from income tax (up to RM1,000 per year of service). Beyond this, employees who retire from an approved company pension scheme at age 55+ qualify for full gratuity exemption (paragraph 30). No existing story models gratuity as a distinct payroll component separate from bonus, records the approved fund membership, or routes the exempted amount correctly to EA Form Part B. The existing 'Approved Pension Scheme' story (US-085) covers the full exemption at retirement; this story covers the RM1,000/year partial exemption applicable to all employees with non-approved or approved fund payments during active service.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Component 'Gratuity' of type Earning exists in fixture with a 'Gratuity Type' option field: (Select: Ex-Gratia / Approved Fund Gratuity / Non-Approved Gratuity)
For 'Approved Fund Gratuity': system calculates the exempt amount as min(total_gratuity, RM1,000 x years_of_service) and the taxable balance
EA Form populates B5 (Gratuity) with total gratuity paid and the exemption amount is shown in the exempt income section (Schedule 6 para 25)
Gratuity Salary Slip line shows: gross gratuity, exempt portion, taxable portion, and the years-of-service multiplier used
PCB calculation excludes the exempt portion from chargeable income for the month the gratuity is paid
Test: 5-year employee receives RM10,000 gratuity from approved fund → exempt RM5,000 (RM1,000 x 5 years); taxable RM5,000 subject to PCB
technicalNotes: ITA 1967, Schedule 6, paragraph 25: gratuity from approved fund — RM1,000 per year of service exemption
ITA 1967, Schedule 6, paragraph 30: full exemption for approved scheme at retirement age 55+ (covered by US-085)
Employee record needs 'Date Joined Company' for years-of-service calculation; if date not set, system should warn
EA Form Part B: gratuity reported in B5 (Imbuhan); exempt portion not included in chargeable employment income
LHDN Public Ruling: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/legislation/public-rulings/ — search for 'gratuity' and 'retirement'

userStories.title: Pre-Submission e-PCB Plus Employee TIN and Tax Category Completeness Validation
userStories.description: LHDN announced that the e-PCB Plus system will outright reject CP39 submissions where any employee record is missing a valid TIN or tax category code. Unlike the existing per-employee on-save NRIC/TIN validation, this is a batch-level gate that must run before the payroll period is closed and the e-CP39 file is generated. Employers need a blocking report listing all employees in the current payroll cycle who are missing required fields so HR can resolve them before submission.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Run e-PCB Plus Pre-flight Check' button is available on the Payroll Period or Salary Slip List view
The check queries all Salary Slips in the selected period and flags employees missing: TIN (custom_lhdn_tin), PCB Category (1/2/3), and IC/Passport type
A blocking dialog is shown with the list of non-compliant employees; the e-CP39 generation action is disabled until all issues are resolved
A report titled 'e-PCB Plus Employee Data Gaps' is accessible from LHDN Developer Tools or the payroll workspace
Resolved employees are automatically excluded from the gap report on rerun
The pre-flight check can be scheduled as a reminder email to HR Manager 5 days before payroll close date
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus rejects the entire batch if any employee row is missing TIN — individual row errors are not allowed
Required employee fields for e-PCB Plus: employee TIN, IC type (NRIC/Passport/Army), PCB category code (1/2/3)
Implement as a Frappe whitelisted API that returns a JSON list of non-compliant employees
Reference: LHDN advisory that employers update TIN details in payroll systems before e-PCB Plus launch
dependencies: Add PCB Category Code (1/2/3) on Employee with CP39 and EA Form integration
Fix CP39 CSV Format — Add All Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns
Implement e-CP39 API Submission to LHDN MyTax / e-PCB Plus

userStories.title: Foreign Worker EPF Oct 2025 Mandate: Domestic Servant Category Exclusion Flag
userStories.description: The October 2025 EPF mandatory contribution for foreign workers explicitly excludes domestic servants (maids, cooks, cleaners, drivers, and gardeners employed in a private household). Employers who employ foreign domestic staff must not deduct or remit EPF at 2% for this category. The ERPNext system must allow flagging a foreign employee's job type as 'Domestic Servant' so the payroll engine skips EPF calculation for them, preventing over-contribution and incorrect i-Akaun CSV entries.
acceptanceCriteria: A checkbox or select field 'Is Domestic Servant (EPF Excluded)' is added to the Employee doctype via fixture, visible only when custom_is_foreign_worker is checked
PCB/EPF salary component logic skips EPF contribution for employees where this flag is enabled and the employee is a non-Malaysian citizen
The EPF i-Akaun CSV export excludes rows for domestic servant foreign employees entirely
A validation warning is shown when an HR user tries to manually add EPF to a domestic servant foreign employee's salary slip
Unit test asserts zero EPF on a domestic servant foreign employee slip after Oct 2025 effective date
The flag defaults to False for all existing foreign employees (non-breaking change)
technicalNotes: Defined exclusion categories per KWSP mandate: domestic servants including maids, cooks, cleaners, drivers, and gardeners employed in private households
Foreign workers on valid Employment Pass (EP) or Temporary Employment Visit Pass (PLKS) are covered; domestic workers typically hold Domestic Helper Pass
Effective date: October 2025 wage month, contribution due 15 November 2025
Add custom_is_domestic_servant Boolean field to Employee via custom_field.json fixture
KWSP circular reference: mandatory-contribution page on kwsp.gov.my
dependencies: Implement Foreign Worker EPF Mandatory Contribution (Effective October 2025)
Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File

userStories.title: EPF i-Akaun CSV: Employee Citizenship Type Code for Malaysian vs Foreign Worker 2% Rate Differentiation
userStories.description: From October 2025, the EPF i-Akaun CSV upload must correctly differentiate between Malaysian employees (11%/12-13% rates) and foreign worker employees (2%/2% rates) using the citizenship type code in the contribution file. The existing EPF CSV generator uses a single rate calculation path. The file format requires a specific column distinguishing Malaysian from non-Malaysian employees so KWSP can apply the correct rate table. Without this, foreign worker contributions may be processed at the wrong rate or rejected.
acceptanceCriteria: The EPF i-Akaun CSV export includes a 'Citizen Type' column with values: '1' for Malaysian and '2' for non-Malaysian
For non-Malaysian employees, the employee contribution amount reflects the 2% rate and employer contribution reflects the 2% rate
For Malaysian employees, existing rates (11% employee, 12% or 13% employer) remain unchanged
Domestic servant foreign employees (with the exclusion flag set) are omitted from the CSV
A test asserts that a payroll run with both Malaysian and foreign employees produces a CSV with correct rate and citizenship columns for each
The CSV format matches the KWSP i-Akaun portal current upload specification for mixed-nationality payrolls
technicalNotes: KWSP i-Akaun CSV format: EPF_iAkaun.csv — row fields include employer code, period, employee identifier, employee contribution amount, employer contribution amount, and employee type
The citizenship differentiation may be achieved via a 'Membership Type' column or by the employee identifier type (NRIC = Malaysian, Passport/FW = Foreign)
Foreign worker EPF rates effective Oct 2025: employee 2%, employer 2%
Verify current KWSP i-Akaun portal upload spec for exact column positions
Reference: payrollpanda.my i-Akaun CSV upload guide and kwsp.gov.my employer mandatory contribution page
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File
Implement Foreign Worker EPF Mandatory Contribution (Effective October 2025)
Foreign Worker EPF Oct 2025 Mandate: Domestic Servant Category Exclusion Flag

userStories.title: Generate PERKESO Borang 34 Accident Notification Within 48-Hour Statutory Deadline
userStories.description: Under the Employees' Social Security Act 1969, employers must report any workplace accident resulting in injury, disease, or death to SOCSO (PERKESO) within 48 hours using Borang 34 (Notice and Benefit Claim Form). The form requires employee personal details, employer SOCSO code, accident description, and a 6-month wage history for benefit calculation. Non-compliance is a criminal offence. ERPNext must surface a Borang 34 generation task the moment a workplace accident is logged so HR can file within the window.
acceptanceCriteria: A new 'Workplace Accident' DocType captures: incident date/time, employee, injury description, accident location, and supervisor witness name
On saving the accident record, system auto-computes the 48-hour statutory deadline and creates a high-priority Task assigned to the HR Manager role
Borang 34 PDF auto-populates with: employee name, NRIC, SOCSO number, job title, employer PERKESO code, accident description, and 6-month average gross wages derived from Salary Slip records
Task status escalates to 'Overdue' if Borang 34 is not marked 'Submitted' within 46 hours of accident logging
A separate '6-Month Wage History' attachment is auto-generated per Borang 34 requirements showing monthly gross wages for the 6 months prior to the accident
Submitted Borang 34 records are retained and linked to the employee for PERKESO audit purposes
technicalNotes: PERKESO Borang 34 PDF layout: assist.perkeso.gov.my — employer logs and submits claims here
Employer PERKESO code stored on Company; employee SOCSO number stored on Employee custom field
6-month wage average is used by PERKESO to compute the Temporary Disablement Benefit daily rate
Employers can submit Borang 34 online via PERKESO ASSIST portal after logging in
Separate claim form may be needed for Permanent Disablement or Dependent Benefit (death) — out of scope here

userStories.title: Employment Pass Minimum Salary Threshold Validator (June 2026 Revised EP Policy)
userStories.description: Malaysia's Expatriate Services Division (ESD) announced revised Employment Pass (EP) minimum salary thresholds effective 1 June 2026 following Cabinet approval on 17 October 2025: Category I increases from RM10,000 to RM20,000; Category II from RM5,000–RM9,999 to RM10,000–RM19,999; Category III from RM3,000–RM4,999 to RM5,000–RM9,999. Employers who pay expatriate employees below the new category threshold risk EP renewal rejection and immigration penalties. ERPNext must validate expatriate salary against the correct EP category minimum before each Salary Slip submission.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType has fields: 'Employment Pass Category' (Cat I / Cat II / Cat III / Not Applicable), 'Employment Pass Number', and 'EP Expiry Date'
A configurable 'EP Salary Threshold' table stores category minimums with effective dates (supporting future revisions without code changes)
On Salary Slip submission for an EP holder where period_end >= 2026-06-01, if gross salary is below the category minimum, submission is blocked with a clear warning citing the ESD policy
HR can record an override justification (e.g., 'Salary increment letter dated X pending MyTax update'), which is logged to the audit trail
A report 'Expatriate EP Salary Compliance' lists all EP holders, their category, current salary, category minimum, and compliance status
EP holders approaching expiry within 90 days are surfaced in the compliance dashboard for proactive renewal
technicalNotes: Official ESD announcement: esd.imi.gov.my/portal/latest-news/announcement/announcement-266-ep-salary-policy-2026
Category II EP applications also require JTKSM approval (Jabatan Tenaga Kerja Semenanjung Malaysia) under S.60K
Category II and III require a Local Succession Plan submitted to ESD from June 1, 2026
Store EP category thresholds in a DocType to allow update without code deployment when MOHA revises again

userStories.title: Auto-Generate TP3 YTD Income Statement for Departing Employees
userStories.description: LHDN's TP3 form allows a new employer to factor a departing employee's year-to-date income from their previous employer into the PCB calculation. The current PRD covers the incoming TP3 workflow (new hires declare prior-employer YTD). This story covers the outgoing side: when an employee leaves mid-year, the current employer must generate a TP3 data document containing the full YTD earnings, statutory deductions, PCB paid, and approved reliefs so the employee can hand it to their next employer for accurate PCB. Without this, the next employer may under-deduct PCB.
acceptanceCriteria: When an Employee record is set to 'Left' status or a Final Settlement Salary Slip is submitted, a 'Generate Outgoing TP3' button appears on the employee record

Generated TP3 PDF is pre-filled with: current employer name and tax reference number, employee full name and NRIC and tax file number, YTD basic salary, YTD gross allowances, BIK value, EPF employee contribution YTD, SOCSO YTD, EIS YTD, Zakat YTD, CP38 YTD, and PCB deducted YTD
YTD figures aggregate from all submitted Salary Slips between 1 January and the employee's last working day
The generated TP3 uses the official LHDN TP3 layout (form code and current year are printed on the PDF)
System saves the generated TP3 record against the Employee for 7-year retention per LHDN audit requirements
The TP3 can be emailed directly to the departing employee from the record
technicalNotes: TP3 is NOT submitted to LHDN — it is an employer-to-employee document only; stress this in UI
Official TP3 reference: hasil.gov.my/en/employers/employer-payroll-data-specification
Employer should also update CP39 with final-month PCB before generating the TP3 to ensure figures match
PCB YTD on the TP3 must match the cumulative CP39 rows for this employee in the current calendar year
If the employee had a TP3 incoming (from previous employer) on hire, those prior-employer figures should NOT appear on this outgoing TP3 (only current employer income)
dependencies: Implement TP3 Carry-Forward Declaration for New Hires (Prior Employer YTD)

userStories.title: Extend Minimum Wage Validation to Cover Micro-Employers from August 1, 2025
userStories.description: Malaysia's Minimum Wages Order 2022 raised the minimum wage to RM1,700/month effective 1 February 2025 for employers with 5 or more employees. A grace period was granted to micro-employers (1–4 employees) until 1 August 2025, after which RM1,700 applies universally to ALL employers regardless of headcount. The existing PRD story ('Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700') covers the February 2025 implementation for larger employers. This story adds the employer-headcount-sensitive validation so micro-employers are correctly brought into scope on August 1, 2025.
acceptanceCriteria: A configurable 'Minimum Wage Schedule' table stores: effective date, minimum wage amount, and applicable employer headcount threshold (e.g., '2025-02-01: RM1,700 for ≥5 employees'; '2025-08-01: RM1,700 for all')
At Salary Slip creation, system counts active employees on the Company as of the payroll period end date and applies the threshold relevant to that employer's size and the period date
For payroll periods ending 2025-02-01 to 2025-07-31 with employer headcount <5, no minimum wage validation applies (grace period)
For payroll periods ending 2025-08-01 onwards, RM1,700 minimum wage is enforced for all employer sizes
HR can record a MOHR exemption reference number to override the block for approved exceptions
A report lists employees whose fixed components total below RM1,700 grouped by employer size and effective date band
technicalNotes: Source: Malaysia Minimum Wages Order 2022 (Perintah Gaji Minimum 2022) and August 2025 gazette extension
Employer headcount = count of active Employees on Company as of Salary Slip period_end
Allowances, overtime, and bonuses cannot offset the RM1,700 basic wage requirement
Minimum wage for part-time employees is prorated proportionally to actual hours worked
MOHR enforcement contact: Jabatan Tenaga Kerja Semenanjung Malaysia (JTKSM)
dependencies: Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700 (Feb 2025 Amendment)

userStories.title: PCB Late Payment Surcharge Computation and Advance Warning (Section 107C ITA 1967)
userStories.description: Under Section 107C(9) of the Income Tax Act 1967, if an employer fails to remit Monthly Tax Deduction (PCB/MTD) by the 15th of the following month, LHDN automatically imposes a 10% surcharge on the outstanding PCB amount. This surcharge is significant on large payrolls and cannot be waived retrospectively. ERPNext must (1) warn HR proactively before the 15th deadline and (2) compute the expected surcharge when recording a late PCB payment, so that penalty costs are tracked and reconciled against LHDN's assessed amounts.
acceptanceCriteria: Each CP39 submission record has a computed 'PCB Payment Due Date' = 15th of the month following the payroll period (adjusted for weekends/public holidays if applicable)
If CP39 payment status is not 'Paid' by the due date, system flags the record 'Overdue' and computes 10% surcharge on the outstanding PCB total
HR Manager receives an automated email alert 5 working days before the CP39 payment due date with: total PCB amount due, due date, and link to the payment record
When HR records a late payment, system prompts for the actual LHDN-assessed surcharge amount (LHDN may compute differently) and stores both the estimated (10%) and the actual assessed surcharge
A 'PCB Compliance Summary' report includes a 'Late Payment History' tab listing all overdue periods, surcharge estimated vs. actual, and total penalties YTD
Late payment events appear on the Statutory Compliance Calendar with a red overdue indicator
technicalNotes: Source: Income Tax Act 1967, Section 107C(9) — 10% penalty on the amount that ought to have been deducted
Surcharge formula: outstanding_PCB_amount × 0.10 (flat, regardless of days late)
LHDN does not automatically grant a grace period for public holidays or weekends on the 15th — verify with local tax counsel
Store surcharge tracking fields on the CP39 DocType: due_date, payment_date, is_late, estimated_surcharge, actual_surcharge_assessed
The 'Track Statutory Compliance Submission Calendar' story covers calendar display; this story adds the PCB penalty calculation logic
dependencies: Implement e-CP39 API Submission to LHDN MyTax / e-PCB Plus
Track Statutory Compliance Submission Calendar and Auto-Escalate Overdue Filings

userStories.title: LHDN STAMPS Employment Contract Digital Stamp Status Tracker
userStories.description: Under the Stamp Act 1949, employment agreements are chargeable instruments and must be stamped to be legally admissible as evidence. LHDN provides e-Stamping via the STAMPS platform (stamps.hasil.gov.my), allowing employers to digitally stamp employment contracts for RM10 per instrument. Employers who fail to stamp contracts risk documents being inadmissible in court and LHDN penalty assessments for unstamped instruments. ERPNext should track stamping status per employment contract and alert HR to any unstamped contracts.
acceptanceCriteria: An 'Employment Contract' DocType (or extension to Employee) has fields: Stamp Reference Number, Stamp Date, Stamp Amount (RM10), and Stamping Method (STAMPS Digital / Manual Legacy / Pre-STAMPS Legacy)
When a new Employment Contract record is saved without a Stamp Reference Number, system automatically creates a pending task for HR: 'Stamp employment contract via LHDN STAMPS (stamps.hasil.gov.my)'
HR enters the STAMPS reference number and stamp date after completing e-stamping, which resolves the task and marks the contract 'Stamped'
A report 'Unstamped Employment Contracts' lists all active employees whose current contract lacks a valid stamp reference, with days since contract start
Contracts started before the STAMPS era (pre-2021) can be marked 'Legacy Stamped' to suppress alerts
System does not integrate directly with STAMPS (no published API); it provides a clickable link to stamps.hasil.gov.my in the pending task for HR convenience
technicalNotes: STAMPS portal: stamps.hasil.gov.my — employer selects instrument type 'Contract of Employment', pays RM10 via FPX, receives a digital stamp certificate
Fixed stamp duty: RM10 per employment contract under First Schedule, Stamp Act 1949
Stamping obligation applies to the instrument (contract document), not the payroll record — this story only tracks compliance status
Employment contract re-stamping may be required if terms are materially varied (e.g., salary revision with new contract)
LHDN penalty for unstamped instrument: stamp duty + penalty up to 20× the duty amount under Stamp Act 1949 S.47A

userStories.title: Fix failing test: SalarySlipCustomFields — lp07 custom lhdn status field exists
userStories.description: Automated test `test_lp07_custom_lhdn_status_field_exists` is failing with status FAIL. This indicates a regression or missing implementation in the unit:lhdn_payroll suite. LP-07: custom_lhdn_status field exists on Salary Slip.
acceptanceCriteria: Test `tests.unit.lhdn_payroll.test_payroll_doctypes.TestSalarySlipCustomFields.test_lp07_custom_lhdn_status_field_exists` passes without error.

Root cause resolved: 'custom_lhdn_status' not found in ['employee_and_payroll_tab', 'section_break_6', 'employee', 'employee_name', 'company', 'department', 'designation', 'branch', 'column_break_obdl', 'posting_date', 'l
technicalNotes: Test category: unit:lhdn_payroll
Test ID: tests.unit.lhdn_payroll.test_payroll_doctypes.TestSalarySlipCustomFields.test_lp07_custom_lhdn_status_field_exists
Error type: AssertionError
Test description: LP-07: custom_lhdn_status field exists on Salary Slip.

userStories.title: Fix failing test: SalarySlipCustomFields — lp09 epf fields exist
userStories.description: Automated test `test_lp09_epf_fields_exist` is failing with status FAIL. This indicates a regression or missing implementation in the unit:lhdn_payroll suite. LP-09: EPF employee/employer fields exist on Salary Slip.
acceptanceCriteria: Test `tests.unit.lhdn_payroll.test_payroll_doctypes.TestSalarySlipCustomFields.test_lp09_epf_fields_exist` passes without error.
Root cause resolved: False is not true : Expected at least 2 EPF fields, got: []
technicalNotes: Test category: unit:lhdn_payroll
Test ID: tests.unit.lhdn_payroll.test_payroll_doctypes.TestSalarySlipCustomFields.test_lp09_epf_fields_exist
Error type: AssertionError
Test description: LP-09: EPF employee/employer fields exist on Salary Slip.

userStories.title: Display EPF Akaun Fleksibel Three-Account Contribution Split on Payslip
userStories.description: Effective 11 May 2024, EPF contributions are automatically split across three accounts: 75% Akaun Persaraan (retirement), 15% Akaun Sejahtera (well-being), and 10% Akaun Fleksibel (flexible). Employee payslips must show the breakdown per account for transparency and to allow employees to verify their allocations match EPF records. The existing EPF i-Akaun upload file story covers contribution submission but does not address the three-account display on payslips.
acceptanceCriteria: Payslip shows three separate EPF line items: Akaun Persaraan (75%), Akaun Sejahtera (15%), and Akaun Fleksibel (10%) of the total EPF contribution amount
Calculation correctly splits employee contribution (11% or 9% depending on age/salary) and employer contribution (12% or 13%) into the three-account proportions
For employees above age 55 who have not joined the three-account restructuring, payslip shows the two-account legacy split
Three-account breakdown appears in both PDF payslip and Employment Act S.61 payslip export
Unit test verifies RM1,000 employee EPF contribution splits as RM750 / RM150 / RM100 across the three accounts
technicalNotes: KWSP confirmed the 75/15/10 three-account structure applies to all contributions after 11 May 2024
The split ratio is fixed by EPF policy — no employer or employee customisation is permitted
Foreign employees under the 2% mandatory EPF rule (effective October 2025) follow the same three-account split
Implement as computed display fields on Salary Slip — actual routing to sub-accounts is performed by EPF, not employer
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File
Generate Employment Act S.61 Compliant Payslip with All Mandatory Fields

userStories.title: Implement PDPA 2024 Amendment Payroll Data Protection Compliance Module
userStories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Royal Assent October 2024, fully effective 1 June 2025) introduces mandatory Data Protection Officer (DPO) appointment, data breach notification to PDPC within 72 hours, increased fines up to RM1 million, and employee right to data portability. Payroll systems process sensitive personal data (NRIC, salary, bank account, tax file, EPF number) and must implement consent tracking, access audit logs, and breach response workflows. No existing PRD story covers PDPA compliance.
acceptanceCriteria: System records employee consent for payroll data processing (NRIC, salary, bank, EPF, SOCSO numbers) with timestamp and consent version
All payroll data access events (Salary Slip read, export, third-party transmission to LHDN/EPF/SOCSO portals) are logged with user, timestamp, and data category
Company DocType has a DPO contact section (name, email, phone); DPO receives alert emails on high-risk data events such as bulk export or unusual access
Payroll data retention enforcer: system flags Salary Slips older than the configured retention period (default 7 years per MOHR and LHDN requirements) for archival review
A 'Data Subject Request' workflow allows HR Manager to export a single employee's complete payroll data history as a portable JSON/CSV (data portability right)
Unit test confirms an audit log entry is created on every Salary Slip read, print, and email action
technicalNotes: PDPA Amendment Act 2024 — mandatory DPO appointment effective 1 June 2025 for organisations processing large volumes of sensitive personal data
Section 12 of amended PDPA: data controllers AND data processors now have direct PDPA obligations
Breach notification obligation: notify PDPC 'as soon as practicable'; notify affected data subjects if breach likely to cause significant harm
Penalties increased to RM1 million (5x previous maximum); 3 years imprisonment for serious violations
Implement as: (1) Employee Payroll Consent DocType, (2) Frappe audit trail hooks on Salary Slip read/export, (3) Company-level DPO settings tab
dependencies: Implement Payroll Wage Register with 6-Year Retention and LHDN/MOHR Audit Export

userStories.title: Employer Statutory Registration Onboarding Checklist with Deadline Alerts
userStories.description: When a new company begins operations with employees, it faces strict statutory registration deadlines with significant penalties for non-compliance: EPF within 7 working days of first hire (penalty: RM10,000 / 3 years jail), SOCSO within 30 days (penalty: RM5,000 / 2 years jail), LHDN E-Number via e-Daftar within 7 days, EIS automatic with SOCSO, and HRDF from date of liability for 10+ employees. No existing PRD story guides new employers through the registration checklist or alerts on pending/overdue statutory registrations.
acceptanceCriteria: Company DocType has a Statutory Registration tab with fields for EPF Employer Number, SOCSO Employer Number, LHDN E-Number, EIS Registration Number, and HRD Corp Registration Number
When a company has active employees but EPF Employer Number is blank for more than 7 working days from the first employee hire date, system flags as OVERDUE with daily email escalation to HR Manager
When a company has active employees but SOCSO Number is blank for more than 30 days from first hire date, system raises a compliance warning
When Malaysian employee count on a Company crosses 10, system prompts HR Manager to register with HRD Corp
Registration checklist shows status (Registered / Pending / Overdue) for each statutory body with clickable links to official registration portals
Scheduled daily job scans all companies and employees to detect registration gaps
technicalNotes: EPF: Employees Provident Fund Act 1991 — 7 working days from date first employee commences employment
SOCSO: Employees' Social Security Act 1969 Section 7 — register within 30 days of first hire
LHDN e-Daftar: employers must register within 7 days of employing first staff; application cancelled if docs not received in 14 days
HRDF: PSMB Act 2001 Regulation 4(1) — compulsory from date employer becomes liable (10+ Malaysian employees in covered sectors)
Implement as scheduled Frappe job (daily check) with notification escalation pattern

userStories.title: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes
userStories.description: LHDN published the TP1(1/2026) form and MTD Computation Specification 2026, effective January 2026. Key changes that affect PCB calculation: children's life/medical insurance now included under existing insurance relief limits; childcare relief extended to ages 6–12 for registered after-school programmes (previously only age ≤6); autism/learning disability child relief ceiling increased from RM6,000 to RM10,000; domestic tourism and cultural attraction RM1,000 relief added (YA2026–2027 only, time-bounded); vaccine relief expanded to all NPRA-approved vaccines. Tax brackets are unchanged for YA2026. All payroll from January 2026 must use the updated form.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 data entry form includes a 'Children Life/Medical Insurance' sub-field under existing insurance relief (respecting combined RM3,000/RM4,000 ceilings)
Childcare relief field accepts children aged 6–12 enrolled in Ministry of Education-registered after-school care programmes
Autism/learning disability child relief ceiling updated from RM6,000 to RM10,000 in the PCB deduction engine
Domestic tourism and cultural attraction RM1,000 relief field is present, with an expiry guard preventing application after 31 December 2027
Vaccine relief field is no longer restricted to a hardcoded list — accepts any NPRA-approved vaccine
PCB calculation uses updated relief totals from new TP1(1/2026) fields for all payroll from January 2026 onwards
Old YA2025 TP1 records are preserved for historical audit without affecting 2026 PCB computation
technicalNotes: Official TP1(1/2026) form PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/gjuhcbc2/bi-tp1-form-2026.pdf
Official MTD Computation Specification 2026: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf
YA2026 individual income tax brackets are UNCHANGED from YA2025 — only relief items and the TP1 form structure change
Tourism relief is time-bounded (YA2026–2027 only): store effective_from = 2026-01-01 and expires_on = 2027-12-31 as system constants
dependencies: Update PCB TP1 Relief Tables for YA2025 (New Housing Loan Interest Relief and Increased Disability Amounts)

userStories.title: Extend Minimum Wage Enforcement to Apprenticeship Contract Workers (NWCC Amendment 2025)
userStories.description: The National Wages Consultative Council (Amendment) Act 2025, effective 1 August 2025, extended the RM1,700 minimum wage to workers on apprenticeship contracts (6–24 month systematic trade training programmes). Previously, apprentices could legally be paid below minimum wage. All payroll validation must now apply the RM1,700 floor to apprenticeship contract employee types, with penalties up to RM10,000 per affected worker for non-compliance.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee type 'Apprentice' or 'Contract Trainee' triggers minimum wage validation at RM1,700/month on Salary Slip save
A pre-payroll warning is raised when any apprenticeship salary is below RM1,700 before the payroll run is confirmed
Domestic workers remain the sole exempt category — apprentices are NOT exempt from minimum wage
Enforcement effective date is gated to 1 August 2025 — no false positives for historical payroll runs before this date
technicalNotes: Add 'Apprentice' as a recognised contract type in Employee master with a minimum_wage_applies = 1 flag
Reuse existing minimum wage validation service; extend employee_type scope to include Apprentice
NWCC Amendment Act 2025 passed concurrently with the micro-employer universal extension (same gazette date: 1 August 2025)
dependencies: Extend Minimum Wage Validation to Cover Micro-Employers from August 1, 2025

userStories.title: Update EPF i-Akaun Employer Contribution CSV Upload Format for Three-Account (75/15/10) Allocation
userStories.description: EPF restructured from two accounts to three accounts effective 11 May 2024: Akaun Persaraan (75%), Akaun Sejahtera (15%), Akaun Fleksibel (10%). The i-Akaun employer contribution upload file must now include three account allocation amount fields per employee row. Systems still submitting on the old two-account (70/30) basis produce incorrect allocation records in EPF member accounts. This affects all contribution months from May 2024 onwards and includes the October 2025 foreign worker mandatory contributions.
acceptanceCriteria: EPF CSV/TXT upload file generated by the system includes three separate account contribution amount columns per employee row
For standard Malaysian employees, allocation is: Akaun Persaraan = 75%, Akaun Sejahtera = 15%, Akaun Fleksibel = 10% of total contribution
For foreign workers (from October 2025 onwards), same 75/15/10 split is applied to their flat 2%+2% contributions
EPF remittance report in ERPNext shows per-employee breakdown across all three accounts per month
Validation rejects or warns when generated account allocation amounts do not sum to the total contribution amount
technicalNotes: EPF Account Restructuring announcement: https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/account-centre/account-restructuring
The old two-account format (70/30 split) is no longer valid for submissions after 11 May 2024
For employees aged 55+ the split ratio may differ — confirm against the current EPF i-Akaun Employer upload specification document
Existing payslip display story covers the UI (three-account split on payslip); this story covers the CSV upload file format sent to EPF
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File
Display EPF Akaun Fleksibel Three-Account Contribution Split on Payslip

userStories.title: Track Employment Contract Stamp Duty Compliance via e-Duti Setem MyTax (Mandatory from 2026)
userStories.description: From 1 January 2026, the Stamp Duty Self-Assessment System (SAS) mandates that all employment contracts be stamped within 30 days of signing via the new e-Duti Setem portal on MyTax. The old STAMPS portal closed on 31 December 2025. Employment contracts are subject to a fixed RM10 stamp duty under Item 4, First Schedule of the Stamp Act 1949. The Finance Bill 2025 increased the exemption threshold to RM3,000/month (contracts for employees earning RM3,000/month or less are stamp duty exempt from 1 January 2026). LHDN will conduct widespread audits covering instruments from 2022–2024. Payroll systems must flag newly-signed contracts requiring stamping and track stamping status to ensure audit readiness.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee master has an 'Employment Contract' section with: contract signing date, gross monthly salary at time of signing, stamp duty exemption flag (auto-set to true if salary ≤ RM3,000), e-Duti Setem stamp reference number, and stamping date
When a new employment contract is recorded with gross salary > RM3,000/month and no stamping reference is set within 30 days of contract date, a compliance alert is sent to HR Manager
A 'Contracts Pending Stamping' report lists all employees with unsigned/unstamped contracts within the 30-day window
Contracts dated 1 January 2025–31 December 2025 that were stamped by 31 December 2025 are correctly flagged as compliant with no penalty
Contracts for employees earning ≤ RM3,000/month are auto-marked as exempt and excluded from stamp duty tracking
technicalNotes: Stamp Duty SAS effective date: 1 January 2026 — e-Duti Setem via MyTax portal (mytax.hasil.gov.my)
Fixed stamp duty: RM10 per contract under Item 4, First Schedule of the Stamp Act 1949
Late stamping penalties: 31–90 days = RM50 or 10% (whichever is higher); >90 days = RM100 or 20% (whichever is higher)
2026 grace year: LHDN will not impose penalties for late stamping on applications submitted 1 January–31 December 2026
Exemption threshold raised from RM300 to RM3,000/month effective 1 January 2026 by Finance Bill 2025
Crowe Malaysia guide: https://www.crowe.com/my/insights/redefining-compliance---stamp-duty-under-malaysias-self-assessment-regime

userStories.title: Apply LHDN PCB 2025 Specification Amendment — Revised TP1 Deduction Items and OKU Relief Update
userStories.description: LHDN published the official 2025 PCB Computerised Calculation Method Specification amendment (Spesifikasi Kaedah Pengiraan Berkomputer PCB 2025). The amendment includes updates to TP1 deduction items, an increase in disability (OKU) tax relief amounts, expanded scope of medical treatment relief, and a new food waste composting machine relief (RM2,500 permanent cap). These changes must be verified against the current PCB calculation engine to ensure the YA2025 computation is accurate before any Year-End EA Form generation or Borang E submission.
acceptanceCriteria: PCB computation for YA2025 includes updated OKU disability relief amounts as per the 2025 spec amendment
Medical treatment relief scope is updated to reflect the 2025 expansion (confirm exact items from spec document)
Food waste composting machine relief (RM2,500, permanent limit) is added as a TP1 line item for YA2025
EV charging facility relief scope expansion is applied as per 2025 spec amendment
Unit test: employee with OKU status and composting machine relief — PCB reflects both correctly for YA2025
PCB calculation engine version is tagged as '2025 Spec Compliant' in system settings for audit reference
technicalNotes: Official document: Spesifikasi Kaedah Pengiraan Berkomputer PCB 2025 (hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf)
Amendment is effective for YA2025 computations; MTD formula itself is unchanged
Changes include: TP1(2024)/TP3(2024) form amendments for deduction items
Distinct from YA2026 Budget changes story — this covers the official 2025 technical spec, not Budget 2026 proposals
Priority: must be applied before EA Form 2025 generation season (EA form due March 1, 2026)
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: Update Employment Contract Stamp Duty Exemption Threshold to RM3,000 Monthly Wage (Budget 2026)
userStories.description: Malaysia's Finance Bill 2025 (Budget 2026) raises the monthly wage exemption threshold for employment contract stamp duty from RM300 to RM3,000, effective 1 January 2026. Under the new self-assessment e-Duti Setem (e-DS) system launching on the same date, employers must self-assess, determine whether each new contract is exempt, and stamp dutiable contracts within 30 days of signing via MyTax. The existing stamp duty tracker must be updated to apply the new RM3,000 threshold for all contracts executed from 1 January 2026 onwards.
acceptanceCriteria: Employment contracts for employees with monthly gross salary ≤RM3,000 are classified as EXEMPT from stamp duty for contracts dated on/after 1 January 2026
Employment contracts for employees with monthly gross salary >RM3,000 are classified as DUTIABLE (RM10 stamp duty per copy) for contracts dated on/after 1 January 2026
Contracts dated before 1 January 2026 continue to use the old RM300 exemption threshold
Payroll onboarding workflow shows stamp duty status (Exempt/Dutiable) automatically based on salary and contract date
For dutiable contracts, system generates a 30-day deadline alert from the contract signing date for e-Duti Setem submission
Threshold constant is configurable in system settings with effective-from date so future changes require no code change
technicalNotes: Finance Bill 2025 / Budget 2026: stamp duty exemption threshold raised from RM300 to RM3,000 monthly wage — effective 1 January 2026
e-Duti Setem (e-DS) self-assessment system launches 15 December 2025, mandatory from 1 January 2026
Stamp duty amount: RM10 per copy of employment contract (Item 4, First Schedule, Stamp Act 1949)
30-day stamping deadline from signing date; late stamping attracts penalty from 1 January 2026 under self-assessment regime
Relevant to the existing 'LHDN STAMPS Employment Contract Digital Stamp Status Tracker' story — update threshold logic
dependencies: Track Employment Contract Stamp Duty Compliance via e-Duti Setem MyTax (Mandatory from 2026)

userStories.title: PDPA 2024 DPO Appointment Registry and 72-Hour Payroll Data Breach Notification Workflow
userStories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024, effective 1 June 2025, requires employers processing large-scale employee payroll data to (a) appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO), (b) notify the Commissioner of the DPO appointment within 21 days, (c) upon detecting a payroll data breach, notify the Commissioner within 72 hours and affected employees within 7 days. These are materially different obligations from general PDPA compliance — the existing module covers data portability and access rights, but not the DPO appointment record-keeping or breach incident management workflows mandated by the February 2025 Commissioner Guidelines.
acceptanceCriteria: Company record stores DPO name, contact email, phone number, appointment date, and Commissioner registration date
System alerts HR Admin if 21-day DPO Commissioner notification deadline not recorded after DPO appointment date

New 'Payroll Data Breach Incident' DocType with fields: discovery_datetime, breach_type, affected_records_count, data_categories_affected, risk_assessment, commissioner_notified_datetime, employee_notification_datetime
72-hour countdown alert activates upon incident creation; red critical alert displayed if commissioner_notified_datetime is blank and 72 hours have elapsed since discovery_datetime
System generates a pre-filled Commissioner Notification Letter (PDF) with breach details, number of affected employees, and risk assessment summary
System generates pre-filled Employee Breach Notification email template once Commissioner has been notified (7-day deadline from Commissioner notification)
Audit log records all breach incident updates with user and timestamp
technicalNotes: New DocType `PDPA Breach Incident` linked to Company; status flow: Draft → Commissioner Notified → Employees Notified → Resolved
DPO fields on Company doctype in `PDPA Compliance` tab via custom field fixture
Commissioner notification required within 72 hours of breach awareness (not breach occurrence) per Guideline on Data Breach Notification (Feb 2025)
Employee notification required within 7 days of Commissioner notification — only required if breach poses significant harm to data subjects
Guideline published: https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/en/ — Guideline on Data Breach Notification and DPO Appointment

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60F(2): Track 60-Day Hospitalization Leave Quota Separately from Ordinary Sick Leave
userStories.description: The Employment Act 1955 S.60F provides two distinct sick leave entitlements: (1) ordinary paid sick leave of 14-22 days based on service years, and (2) additional hospital/in-patient leave of up to 60 days per year when an employee is hospitalized or requires leave following hospitalization as certified by a registered medical practitioner. These must be tracked separately — hospitalization leave does not reduce ordinary sick leave entitlement. The existing 'Track Employment Act S.60F Sick Leave Entitlement per Service Band' story covers ordinary sick leave; this story addresses the distinct 60-day hospitalization leave quota.
acceptanceCriteria: Two distinct Leave Types: 'Sick Leave (EA)' for ordinary sick leave (14-22 days by service band) and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA)' for in-patient/post-hospitalization (60 days annual)
Hospitalization Leave allocation of 60 days auto-created for all EA-covered employees at each leave year start
Hospitalization Leave application requires: `hospitalization_discharge_date` (Date) and `medical_certificate_type` (Select: Hospitalization Certificate | Post-Hospitalization Medical Advice)
Leave balance summary on payslip shows both balances separately: 'Sick Leave (EA): X days' and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA): Y days'
System validates Hospitalization Leave applications are processed against the 60-day quota, not the ordinary sick leave quota
Alert to HR if employee Hospitalization Leave balance reaches zero — no further hospitalization leave available for the year
technicalNotes: EA S.60F(2): Up to 60 days paid hospitalization leave when certified by registered medical practitioner that in-patient care is necessary
EA S.60F(1): Ordinary sick leave: 14 days (<2 years service), 18 days (2-5 years), 22 days (>5 years)
Both entitlements paid at ordinary rate of pay
Employee may use both entitlements in same year — they are separate pools
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60F — https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265).pdf

userStories.title: PCB 2026 Specification: Enforce Truncation-then-5-Cent-Ceiling Rounding Rule in MTD Calculation Engine
userStories.description: The official LHDN PCB 2026 Computerised Calculation Specification mandates a specific two-step rounding rule that is different from standard mathematical rounding: Step 1 — truncate the raw PCB amount to exactly 2 decimal places (dropping subsequent digits without rounding); Step 2 — apply ceiling rounding up to the nearest 5 cents. For example: 123.023 → truncated to 123.02 → rounded to 123.05; and 123.061 → truncated to 123.06 → rounded to 123.10. If the current MTD engine uses standard banker's rounding or simple 2dp rounding, it will produce incorrect PCB values that diverge from LHDN's expected amounts, potentially causing CP39 submission mismatches.
acceptanceCriteria: MTD calculation engine applies two-step rounding as final step: truncated = floor(raw_pcb * 100) / 100; final_pcb = ceil(truncated / 0.05) * 0.05
All PCB calculation paths (Method 1 regular salary and Method 2 YTD recalculation) pass through the same rounding function
CP39 CSV output values are in 2dp format matching the 5-cent rounded amounts
Dedicated unit test class `TestPCB2026Rounding` covers at least 15 representative scenarios including edge cases at 0.00, 0.01, 0.05, 0.06 fractional values
Rounding function is extracted into a shared utility `pcb_utils.round_pcb(amount)` callable from all calculation paths
technicalNotes: From LHDN PCB 2026 Spec: 'Pengiraan berkomputer PCB hendaklah dalam dua titik perpuluhan dan mesti mengabaikan sebarang angka berikutnya (cth: 123.023 ≈ 123.02). Dari situ, amaun perlu dibundarkan kepada 5 sen yang paling hampir ke atas; cth: 123.02 ≈ 123.05 manakala 123.06 ≈ 123.10'
Use Python Decimal module to avoid floating-point precision errors: from decimal import Decimal, ROUND_DOWN
Step 1: truncated = Decimal(str(raw)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'), rounding=ROUND_DOWN)
Step 2: import math; final = math.ceil(float(truncated) / 0.05) * 0.05; final = Decimal(str(final)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'))
Reference: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf

userStories.title: Enforce e-CP22 Online-Only Mandatory Submission and MyTax Employer Representative Authorization Check
userStories.description: From 1 September 2024, LHDN mandated that all CP22 new employee notifications must be submitted exclusively through the e-CP22 system on MyTax Portal — manual paper submissions to LHDN offices are no longer accepted. Critically, the submission can only be made by the company director (with MyTax Employer role) or a registered Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan). The existing 'Auto-Submit CP22 via e-CP22 MyTax Within 30 Days' story focuses on the 30-day deadline tracking; this story enforces the online-only mandate, validates Employer Representative setup, and blocks paper CP22 generation.
acceptanceCriteria: CP22 data generation produces only a digital summary for manual MyTax upload — no PDF formatted for paper/postal submission to LHDN office
Company record stores MyTax Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan): name, MyTax login ID, authorization date — required field before e-CP22 workflow is accessible
Alert displayed: 'e-CP22 must be submitted by company director or registered Wakil Majikan via mytax.hasil.gov.my — system cannot auto-submit'
After manual submission, HR records LHDN acknowledgement reference number on the Employee record (this reference is visible in MyTax after submission)
System blocks new employee onboarding finalization if CP22 status = 'Pending' and 30 days have elapsed since hire date
System shows penalty notice when 30-day deadline at risk: 'Offence under ITA 1967 S.83(2) — Fine RM200 to RM20,000 or 6 months imprisonment or both'
technicalNotes: Mandatory online-only e-CP22 submission effective: 1 September 2024
Authorized submitters: Company Director (MyTax Employer role) OR registered Wakil Majikan — not HR staff without MyTax authorization
LHDN issues acknowledgement reference upon successful e-CP22 submission — must be stored on Employee record for audit
Manual CP22 paper form no longer valid after September 2024 — LHDN offices will not accept them
Reference: https://landco.my/social/cp22-online/ and https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/notifications-of-new-employee/

userStories.title: Fix failing test: BrowserUIRegressions — reg14 lhdn dev tools getpage returns script
userStories.description:
Automated test `test_reg14_lhdn_dev_tools_getpage_returns_script` is failing with status FAIL. This indicates a regression or missing implementation in the regression suite. REG-14: LHDN Dev Tools page getpage response includes page script.

userStories.title: PCB Under-Deduction Employer Liability Alert (Section 107A ITA 1967)
userStories.description: Under Section 107A of the Income Tax Act 1967, employers are personally liable for any shortfall in monthly PCB deductions — if an employee's PCB is under-deducted, LHDN can assess the employer for the full uncollected tax plus penalties. The payroll system must detect anomalous PCB under-deduction patterns before Salary Slips are finalised and require mandatory payroll admin acknowledgement with documented justification before the slip can proceed.
acceptanceCriteria: System flags a Salary Slip where PCB drops more than 50% vs. the prior month without a corresponding documented change in income or declared relief values
Alert raised when an employee's computed PCB is zero but their chargeable income (after EPF and declared reliefs) exceeds the applicable zero-tax threshold
Alert is displayed as a mandatory acknowledgement dialog that must be completed before the Salary Slip can be submitted
Payroll admin acknowledgement reason is captured and stored in the PCB Change Audit Trail DocType with timestamp and user
Monthly compliance report summarises all flagged and acknowledged under-deduction cases for LHDN audit readiness
technicalNotes: Section 107A ITA 1967: employer personally liable for uncollected PCB if employee later cannot pay
Zero-tax threshold for single monthly-salaried employee: chargeable income ≤ RM2,851/month after EPF and standard RM9,000 individual relief annualised and monthly-ised
Threshold: flag if PCB drops >50% month-over-month without matching income/relief change event
Complements existing 'Implement PCB Change Audit Trail DocType' story
LHDN MTD Payment Guidelines: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/mtd-payment/
dependencies: Implement PCB Change Audit Trail DocType
Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)

userStories.title: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025
userStories.description: The existing minimum wage story covers the RM1,700 requirement effective February 2025 for MASCO employers and those with 5+ employees. However, from 1 August 2025, the RM1,700 minimum wage universally extends to ALL employers regardless of headcount — including employers with fewer than 5 employees who were previously on the RM1,500 rate. Non-compliance carries fines up to RM10,000 per affected employee per offence. The payroll system must update its minimum wage validation logic to apply RM1,700 universally from August 2025 payroll, removing any employee-count conditional.
acceptanceCriteria: Minimum wage validation in payroll uses RM1,700 as the floor for ALL employees regardless of employer headcount, effective for payroll periods from August 2025 onward
The existing employee-count threshold conditional (5+ employees for RM1,700 vs <5 for RM1,500) is removed from the validation engine for payroll periods >= 2025-08-01
A one-time migration alert notifies HR of any employees currently paid between RM1,500 and RM1,699.99 who will be in violation from August 2025
Payslip submission for August 2025 or later with basic salary below RM1,700 raises a hard validation error citing 'Minimum Wages Order 2024 — universal enforcement from 1 August 2025'
The penalty warning text is updated to reflect RM10,000 per employee fine for non-compliance
technicalNotes: Minimum Wages Order 2024: RM1,700 universal effective 1 August 2025 per MOHR gazette
Previously: RM1,700 applied to MASCO employers from Feb 2025 and employers with 5+ from Feb 2025; <5 non-MASCO employers had until Aug 2025 grace
Foreign workers are covered; domestic workers remain exempt
Reference: MOHR announcement (https://pandahrms.com/rm1700-minimum-wage-applies-to-all-employers-starting-1-august-2025/)
dependencies: Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700 (Feb 2025 Amendment)

userStories.title: EIS Amendment 2025 — Payroll Validation: Prohibit Employer EIS Contribution Recovery from Employee Wages
userStories.description: The EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 explicitly prohibits employers from recovering their share of EIS contributions from employee wages, clarifying that only the employee's share (0.2% capped at RM6,000 wage ceiling) may appear as a deduction on payslips. Some employers had been improperly charging the employer's 0.4% EIS share to employees. The payroll engine must enforce a validation rule that rejects any salary deduction component tagged as employer-side EIS recovery.
acceptanceCriteria: Payroll validation raises a blocking error if any salary deduction component has both custom_is_eis_component = 1 and is_employer_contribution = 1
The LHDN Payroll Compliance Report shows separate rows for employer EIS (0.4%) and employee EIS (0.2%) with column 'Recoverable from Employee?' = No / Yes respectively
Existing salary structures containing employer EIS deduction components are flagged in a pre-migration report for HR review
Unit test asserts that a Salary Slip with an incorrectly added employer EIS deduction component raises a validation exception before submission
technicalNotes: EIS rates: employer 0.4%, employee 0.2% — both capped at RM6,000 wage ceiling (effective Oct 2024)
EIS (Amendment) Bill 2025 reinforces that only the employee share may be deducted from salary — employer share is a company cost
Salary Component doctype: check the combination of custom_is_eis_component and is_employer_contribution to detect illegal cross-recovery
This rule applies equally to the SOCSO employer contribution share (already prohibited under SOCSO Act)
dependencies: Generate SOCSO and EIS e-Caruman Upload File (PERKESO ASSIST Portal)

userStories.title: Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)
userStories.description: PSMB Act 2001 requires HRD Corp levy to be computed on wages which includes basic salary and fixed allowances (housing, transport, shift allowance) but excludes overtime, bonuses, commissions, service charges, and reimbursements. The existing HRDF stories cover the 1%/0.5% rate and Borang PSMB/6 generation, but no story classifies salary components into levy-base vs. non-levy-base, nor validates that the levy is computed on the correct base each month.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary component configuration has a boolean field 'Is HRDF Levy Base' (default: True for fixed allowances, False for variable/performance components)
HRDF levy calculated as: sum of components where is_hrdf_levy_base = True, multiplied by levy_rate (1% or 0.5%)
Monthly Salary Slip shows HRDF levy base amount and levy contribution computed
HRDF levy base excludes: overtime pay, bonus, commission, service charge, gratuity, one-time payments
HRDF levy base includes: basic salary, housing allowance (fixed), transport allowance (fixed), shift allowance (fixed)
Report shows monthly HRDF levy base vs. gross wages so HR can audit classification
technicalNotes: PSMB Act 2001 definition of wages for levy: Section 2 interpretation and Third Schedule
HRD Corp levy contribution guide: https://hrdcorp.gov.my/faq
Add 'is_hrdf_levy_base' custom field to Salary Component via fixture
Monthly levy payment due to HRD Corp by 15th of following month via HRD Corp employer portal
dependencies: Add HRDF levy validation and basic reporting
Fix HRDF Levy Rate - 1% Mandatory for All Employers with 10+ Employees

userStories.title: Implement DTA 183-Day Residency Tracker and Short-Term Assignee PCB Exemption Workflow
userStories.description: Malaysia's Double Taxation Agreements (DTAs) with 70+ countries include a short-term employment exemption: non-citizen employees from DTA countries working in Malaysia for fewer than 183 days in a calendar year may be exempt from Malaysian income tax on employment income. Payroll systems must track days-in-Malaysia, flag employees approaching 183 days, and support a PCB exemption override. The existing 'Implement Expatriate Gross-Up Calculator and DTA Country Table' story covers grossed-up salary for tax residents; this story covers the pre-183-day exemption tracking workflow.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record has Tax Residency Status field: Resident, Non-Resident, Short-Term Assignee (DTA Exempt)
Days-in-Malaysia counter tracks calendar year presence from a structured Travel Log sub-table (date, direction: Entry/Exit)
System generates alert when Short-Term Assignee exceeds 150 days in-Malaysia in a calendar year (approaching 183-day threshold)
PCB exemption flag on Salary Slip suppresses PCB deduction and generates a zero-PCB CP39 record for that employee
When Short-Term Assignee exceeds 183 days, system automatically removes exemption flag and triggers backpay-of-PCB calculation for prior months in the same year
DTA country table lists treaty partner countries; system validates that employee home country has an active DTA before allowing exemption flag
technicalNotes: LHDN DTA page: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/international/double-taxation-avoidance-agreement-dtadtaa/
Income Tax Act 1967, Section 13(2)(h): employment income exemption under DTA for short-term assignments
183 days counted per calendar year (1 Jan to 31 Dec), including partial days of entry and exit
Employer must retain documentation for treaty claim and may need to file LHDN exemption notification in some DTA scenarios
dependencies: Implement Expatriate Gross-Up Calculator and DTA Country Table

userStories.title: Implement Commission-as-Normal-Remuneration MTD Formula for Commission-Heavy Pay Structures
userStories.description: LHDN PCB specification distinguishes between commission treated as 'normal remuneration' (received monthly alongside or instead of salary) versus commission treated as 'additional/irregular remuneration' (received ad-hoc on top of regular salary). When commission is the primary or only monthly income, it is classified as normal remuneration and uses the standard monthly PCB annualization formula — not the bonus lump-sum formula. Payroll systems that always route commission through the bonus formula will over-deduct PCB for commission-only salespeople or underpay for irregular commissions. No existing story captures the commission-type classification field or the formula switch logic.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Component DocType has a field 'Commission Classification' (Select: Normal Remuneration / Additional Remuneration) visible when component type = Earning and commission-related

PCB calculation applies: if Commission Classification = Normal Remuneration, annualize as (salary + commission) x 12 using Method 1; if Additional Remuneration, apply the irregular payment formula from LHDN PCB spec Section 3.2
Employee record allows overriding default classification via 'Commission Type Override' field for cases where the same component type is used differently per employee
Salary Slip shows computed PCB with a tooltip indicating which formula was applied (Normal or Additional)

Tests: commission-only employee with RM5,000/month commission → PCB using standard formula; same employee receives RM20,000 year-end performance bonus → additional remuneration formula for that month only
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB Specification 2026, Section 3: 'Imbuhan Tetap' (Fixed Remuneration) vs 'Imbuhan Tambahan' (Additional Remuneration) classification
PCB Spec reference: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf
Normal remuneration commission: monthly PCB = [(P - M) x R + B - (Z + X)] / remaining_months where P includes commission
Additional remuneration formula: PCB = [(P + (commission/12) - M) x R + B - (Z + X)] - existing PCB already paid
Talenox guide on commission PCB: https://blog.talenox.com/malaysia-tax-guide-how-do-i-calculate-pcb-mtd-part-2-of-3/
dependencies: Implement PCB/MTD monthly calculation validation (US-004)

userStories.title: HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File and HRDF Employer Portal Integration
userStories.description: PSMB Act 2001 requires registered employers (10+ Malaysian employees mandatory at 1%; 5-9 employees optional at 0.5%) to pay HRD Corp levy by the 15th of each following month via the HRD Corp employer portal. The levy base is basic salary plus fixed allowances (excluding overtime, bonus, commissions). The existing HRDF stories cover rate determination and Borang PSMB/6 annual return. A missing operational gap is the generation of a monthly HRDF levy payment summary file (per the HRD Corp portal format) that payroll staff can upload directly, analogous to how EPF Borang A and SOCSO Borang 8A are auto-generated.
acceptanceCriteria: Monthly HRDF Levy Payment report generates a summary: company HRD Corp registration number, month, total levy-base wages, applicable levy rate (1% or 0.5%), total levy amount payable
Per-employee detail breakdown shows: employee name, NRIC, levy-base wage components, levy amount for that employee
Report output includes both a human-readable PDF and an upload-compatible text/CSV file in HRD Corp portal format (if HRD Corp publishes a batch upload specification)
Monthly levy payment due date (15th of following month) tracked in the Statutory Compliance Submission calendar
Levy calculation cross-checks against the HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier to ensure only levy-base components are included
Test: employee with RM4,000 basic + RM500 fixed transport + RM800 OT → levy base = RM4,500; levy = RM45 at 1%
technicalNotes: PSMB Act 2001, levy payment obligation: https://hrdcorp.gov.my/faq
HRD Corp employer portal for levy payment: https://www.hrdcorp.gov.my
Levy due date: 15th of the following month; late payment attracts interest
Levy base definition: wages = basic salary + fixed allowances; excludes overtime, bonus, commission, reimbursements — per PSMB Act 2001 Section 2 definition of 'wages'
HRD Corp may not publish a machine-readable batch upload spec; if so, the story delivers the PDF summary + manual payment guidance via the LHDN Payroll workspace
dependencies: Add HRDF levy validation and basic reporting
Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)

userStories.title: Track HRD Corp Levy Utilization Rate and Alert on 15% Under-Utilisation Penalty
userStories.description: From 1 March 2025, HRD Corp imposes a 15% deduction on accumulated unused levy balance for employers with a balance of RM50,000+ AND a utilization rate below 50% of total contributions in the prior calendar year. Employers who fail to claim enough training by year-end face an automatic forfeiture. The system must track the ratio of approved training claims vs. levy contributions per year and warn HR managers when utilization is trending below the 50% threshold.
acceptanceCriteria: A new field or child table on Company or HRD Corp Settings stores year-to-date levy contributions and approved training claims in MYR
A dashboard tile on the LHDN Payroll workspace shows the current utilization percentage for the active calendar year
When utilization rate drops below 60% by 1 October of any year, an automatic email notification is sent to the HR Manager role
A background job computes utilization monthly: (total approved claims YTD) / (total levy contributions YTD) * 100
Report includes the employer's estimated 15% penalty exposure if training claims remain below 50% by year-end
Applies only to employers whose accumulated levy balance exceeds RM50,000
technicalNotes: HRD Corp eTRIS portal (etris.hrdcorp.gov.my) is where training claims are approved — store approved claim amounts manually or via CSV import
Levy base formula: (Basic Salary - Unpaid Leave + Fixed Allowances) x 1% per month
The 15% deduction is applied by HRD Corp directly from the levy balance — no employer action required to trigger it (only to avoid it)
Threshold conditions: levy balance >= RM50,000 AND utilization < 50%; both must be true
Grace period historically runs Jan-Feb; build alert cadence around Q3 warning and year-end final alert
dependencies: HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File and HRDF Employer Portal Integration
Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)

userStories.title: PCB 2025 Spec: Update TP1 Medical Relief Categories — Co-Payment Insurance, Mental Health, and COVID Tests
userStories.description: The LHDN 2025 PCB computation specification added expanded medical treatment expense relief categories that must be reflected in TP1 form options used for PCB calculation. New sub-categories include: medical costs under co-payment insurance/takaful products, mental health consultation fees, COVID-19 self-detection test kit purchases, and self-health monitoring equipment — all within the existing RM10,000 annual cap (with a RM1,000 sub-limit for the exam/COVID/mental health cluster). ERPNext TP1 relief configuration must be updated to present these new categories.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Employee Relief Declaration doctype includes new medical sub-category options: 'Medical (Co-Payment Insurance/Takaful)', 'Mental Health Consultation', 'COVID-19 Self-Test Kits', 'Self-Health Monitoring Equipment'
PCB calculator enforces the RM1,000 sub-limit for the exam/COVID/mental health cluster within the overall RM10,000 medical relief cap
The TP1 Explanatory Notes (2025) source is referenced in field help text for the new categories
EA Form Section B correctly maps each medical sub-category to the appropriate line item
