Unit test verifies that claiming all new sub-categories still caps total medical relief at RM10,000
technicalNotes: Official TP1 Explanatory Notes 2025: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/hkzf5rqd/nota-penerangan-borang-tp1-2025-en.pdf
PCB 2025 Amendment spec PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
Co-payment insurance relief applies when employee medical insurance has a co-payment clause (patient pays a fixed % of bill)
Mental health consultation is a new standalone relief item for FY2025
All amounts claimed by employee on TP1 and factored into PCB monthly calculation by employer
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration
Update PCB TP1 Relief Tables for YA2025 (New Housing Loan Interest Relief and Increased Disability Amounts)

userStories.title: EA Form for Deceased Employee — Prorated Period and Estate Administrator Delivery
userStories.description: When an employee passes away mid-year, the employer is still legally obligated to issue the EA Form (CP8A) for the period the employee worked, to assist the estate administrator in settling the deceased's tax affairs with LHDN. The current EA Form generation logic assumes a full-year or end-of-employment-date cutoff. A deceased employee requires a prorated form with a special notation and delivery to the estate or executor, not the employee. A CP22B (notification of death) must also be submitted via e-SPC within 30 days.
acceptanceCriteria: The EA Form generation supports a 'Cessation Reason' parameter with an option for 'Death'
When Cessation Reason = Death, the EA Form income figures are prorated to the date of death (from system-stored termination date)
The generated EA Form PDF includes a header notation 'Issued to Estate of [Employee Name]' and the cessation date

A checklist reminder is shown to HR when marking an employee as terminated with reason 'Death': (1) Submit CP22B via e-SPC within 30 days, (2) Withhold final salary pending LHDN clearance if required, (3) Issue EA Form to estate administrator
EA Form can be issued to a configurable 'Estate Contact' email address stored on the termination record
Unit test verifies prorated income calculation for an employee who died on day 15 of month 6
technicalNotes: CP22B is the LHDN employer notification form for employee death — mandatory within 30 days of death
Prorated income = sum of all submitted salary slips up to and including the period of death
Final salary withholding: under s.83(3) ITA 1967, employer must withhold wages pending LHDN clearance
e-SPC module on MyTax handles CP22B submission (same portal as CP21 and CP22A)
Reference: https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/notifications-of-termination-of-service
dependencies: Generate EA Form (Borang EA) for employees
Rebuild EA Form to Include All Mandatory LHDN Section A/B/C/D Fields
Auto-Submit CP21/CP22A/CP22B via e-SPC MyTax at Cessation or Departure

userStories.title: HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard
userStories.description: Beyond generating levy payment files, employers must track approved training claims against accumulated levy balance to manage the 15% under-utilisation penalty risk. HRD Corp's eTRIS portal stores approved claims but ERPNext has no visibility into claim history. This story adds a lightweight claim recording feature: HR managers log approved eTRIS claims (date, amount, program name) and the system computes the running utilization ratio, projecting year-end exposure to the 15% penalty.
acceptanceCriteria: A new 'HRD Corp Training Claim' doctype allows recording: claim date, approved amount (MYR), program name, eTRIS claim reference number, and linked Company
A dashboard section on the LHDN Payroll workspace shows: Total Levy YTD, Total Claims Approved YTD, Utilization %, and 15% Penalty Exposure if utilization < 50%
Penalty exposure is computed as: (levy_balance >= 50000 AND utilization < 50%) ? (total_levy_ytd - total_claims_ytd) * 0.15 : 0
A year-end summary report shows month-by-month levy contribution vs. claims for audit purposes
When a new claim record is saved, the dashboard automatically recalculates the utilization ratio
An alert triggers if the employer's levy balance exceeds RM50,000 and utilization is below 50% after 30 September
technicalNotes: eTRIS portal at etris.hrdcorp.gov.my — claims are approved there; ERPNext records them manually or via CSV import from eTRIS export
Levy balance threshold for 15% penalty: RM50,000 and above (introduced as policy from YA2024)
Utilization formula: (sum of approved claims in calendar year) / (sum of levy contributions in calendar year) * 100
The 'levy balance' referred to by HRD Corp is the running accumulated balance, not just the current year's contribution
Reference: HRD Corp FAQ at hrdcorp.gov.my and OTC Training Centre advisory at otc.com.my
dependencies: Track HRD Corp Levy Utilization Rate and Alert on 15% Under-Utilisation Penalty
HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File and HRDF Employer Portal Integration

userStories.title: Enforce Paternity Leave 7-Day Consecutive Pay at ORP with EA Section 37A Eligibility Rules
userStories.description: The Employment (Amendment) Act 2022 Section 37A mandates 7 consecutive days of paid paternity leave at the Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) for eligible male employees. Eligibility: married, employed ≥12 months with current employer, maximum 5 births per employee over the full tenure. The existing PRD story 'Track Maternity/Paternity Leave and Validate Maternity Pay Rate' addresses tracking broadly. This story enforces the specific statutory constraints — consecutive-day requirement, 5-birth cap per employer, 12-month service eligibility check — which are MOHR-auditable requirements with penalty risk if violated.
acceptanceCriteria: Leave Application DocType for 'Paternity Leave' type validates that selected dates are 7 consecutive calendar days and rejects non-consecutive selections with a descriptive error
On paternity leave submission, system checks cumulative approved paternity events for the employee at this employer; if ≥5 previous approved events exist, the new application is blocked as ineligible
System checks employee service duration: if employment start date to leave start date is <12 months, a warning is raised requiring HR override with justification
Salary Slip for a period containing paternity leave auto-includes a 'Paternity Leave Pay' earning line equal to 7 × daily ORP (monthly basic salary ÷ 26)
Paternity leave pay is subject to PCB (unlike maternity allowance which is exempt); the earning component must be flagged as PCB-applicable
All paternity leave events are retained per Employee for MOHR audit inspection with dates and any override justifications
technicalNotes: Source: Employment Act 1955 Section 37A as inserted by Employment (Amendment) Act 2022, effective 1 January 2023
ORP divisor: 26 days for monthly-rated employees per EA Third Schedule; verify against company's custom divisor if configured
Paternity leave applies to married male employees in the private sector — does not apply to civil servants (governed by JPA circulars)
Store paternity leave event count in a child table on Employee with dates, birth certificate reference, and approved/rejected status
5-birth cap is per employer (not lifetime) — if employee switches employers, the count resets at the new employer
dependencies: Track Maternity/Paternity Leave and Validate Maternity Pay Rate
Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

userStories.title: Implement 60-Day PCB Exemption Counter for Short-Term Non-Resident Workers
userStories.description: Under LHDN tax rules, non-resident employees are fully exempt from income tax (and PCB) if their total employment period in Malaysia is 60 days or fewer within a calendar year. This exemption applies to all non-residents regardless of DTA coverage and is a distinct mechanism from the DTA 183-day tracker. When the 60-day threshold is exceeded mid-year, the employee retroactively becomes a taxable non-resident at 30% flat rate. Directors are explicitly excluded from this exemption. Employers must track cumulative working days in Malaysia per non-resident employee.
acceptanceCriteria: Non-resident Employee records have a 'Days in Malaysia (YTD)' counter, auto-incremented from the number of payroll days covered in each submitted Salary Slip
When counter is ≤ 60 days, PCB is set to RM0 and payslip notes 'PCB Exempt — Short-Term Non-Resident (S.8(3) ITA 1967)'
When counter crosses 60 days, system flags the employee for PCB correction: generates a corrected CP39 row at 30% of total YTD gross income and deducts the shortfall from the next payroll run
Directors (where Employee Type = Director) are blocked from 60-day exemption regardless of day count
System raises a pre-emptive alert when a non-resident employee is projected to cross 60 days within the next 5 working days
Unit test: employee with 55 days in Malaysia has RM0 PCB; employee whose 61st working day is processed triggers 30% flat-rate PCB correction
technicalNotes: Section 8(3) of the Income Tax Act 1967 — employment income exempt if employment period in Malaysia is 60 days or less per calendar year
LHDN 'Tax Treatment Residents & Non-Residents' publication explicitly confirms 60-day rule; directors and public entertainers are excluded
Distinct from the DTA 183-day tracker: the 60-day rule is a domestic exemption; DTA exemptions require treaty-country residency certification
When threshold is crossed mid-year, employer must submit corrected CP39 rows for all prior months via e-PCB Plus amendment flow
Days straddling two calendar years are counted independently per year
dependencies: Add non-resident flat-rate 30% tax flag and handling
Implement DTA 183-Day Residency Tracker and Short-Term Assignee PCB Exemption Workflow
e-PCB Plus Prior Month PCB Amendment — Trigger Corrected CP39 Row on Salary Slip Resubmission

userStories.title: Support HRD Corp Voluntary Levy Registration for 5-9 Employee SMEs at 0.5% Rate
userStories.description: Employers with 5–9 Malaysian employees may voluntarily register with HRD Corp and contribute at 0.5% of monthly wages (versus the mandatory 1% for 10+ employees). Voluntary registration grants SMEs full access to training grants and HRD Corp eTRiS subsidies. The existing HRDF implementation in the PRD covers only the mandatory 1% threshold. A voluntary registration pathway with the lower 0.5% rate, SME eligibility prompts, and levy calculation differentiation is required.
acceptanceCriteria: Company HRDF settings includes a 'Registration Type' field with options: Not Registered / Voluntary (5-9 employees, 0.5%) / Mandatory (10+ employees, 1%)
When Malaysian employee count is between 5 and 9 and Registration Type is Not Registered, system prompts HR Manager about optional HRD Corp voluntary registration with a training grant benefits note
HRDF levy calculation uses 0.5% rate when Registration Type is Voluntary and 1% when Mandatory
Levy base is identical for both tiers: basic salary + fixed allowances; excludes overtime, bonus, and commissions
When headcount crosses 10 Malaysian employees, system recommends upgrading Registration Type to Mandatory
Monthly Borang PSMB/6 payment file reflects the correct 0.5% or 1% rate based on Registration Type
technicalNotes: PSMB Act 2001 (Amended 2021): mandatory for 10+ Malaysian employees; voluntary for 5-9 employees at 0.5%
Payment deadline: 15th of following month for both voluntary and mandatory registrations
Voluntary registrants access the same HRD Corp eTRiS grant portal as mandatory contributors
Employers with fewer than 5 Malaysian employees are not eligible for any HRDF registration
Implement as additional enum option in existing HRDF configuration on Company DocType
dependencies: Fix HRDF Levy Rate — 1% Mandatory for All Employers with 10+ Employees
Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)

userStories.title: Enforce 30-Day EPF i-Akaun Registration Deadline Alert for Newly Eligible Foreign Workers (Oct 2025)
userStories.description: Effective October 2025, all foreign workers on valid work passes must contribute to EPF. EPF regulations require employers to register newly eligible foreign employees in i-Akaun Employer within 30 days of the mandatory commencement date (or within 30 days of hire for new foreign workers engaged from October 2025 onwards). Failure to register within the deadline exposes the employer to late registration penalties. The existing foreign worker EPF story covers contribution calculation; this story covers the registration deadline enforcement specifically.
acceptanceCriteria: Existing active foreign workers with hire dates before October 2025 trigger a one-time alert: 'EPF Registration Required by 14 November 2025'
New foreign worker hires from October 2025 onwards trigger a 30-day countdown alert from their Date of Joining for EPF i-Akaun registration
Employee master includes an 'EPF i-Akaun Registration Confirmed' flag with confirmation date for foreign worker records
When 30 days pass without the confirmation flag being set, a high-priority compliance alert is sent to the HR Manager role
Domestic servants (maids, cooks, cleaners, babysitters, drivers) are excluded from EPF registration alerts — no false positives
technicalNotes: EPF mandatory for non-Malaysian citizens: https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/employer/responsibilities/non-malaysian-citizen-employees
EPF announced automatic registration via the work-pass system but employers must still verify registration status in i-Akaun Employer portal
Valid work passes triggering mandatory EPF: Employment Pass, Work Permit, Professional Visit Pass, Residence Pass-Talent
Domestic worker exclusion is in the Third Schedule of the Employees Provident Fund Act 1991
dependencies: Implement Foreign Worker EPF Mandatory Contribution (Effective October 2025)

userStories.title: Implement BIK Motor Vehicle 50% Prescribed Value Discount for Company Cars Over 5 Years Old
userStories.description: LHDN Public Ruling 11/2019 specifies that under the Prescribed Value Method, the annual taxable BIK value of a company motor car — including the associated fuel benefit — is reduced by 50% when the vehicle is more than 5 years old from its original purchase date. Employers frequently misapply the full prescribed value to aged vehicles, over-reporting employee taxable income on EA Forms and PCB calculations. The fuel benefit (RM1,800/year) is halved for qualifying vehicles; the driver benefit (RM7,200/year) is assessed separately and is not discounted.
acceptanceCriteria: Company car record in the BIK module includes a 'Date of Original Registration/Purchase' field for vehicle age computation
BIK prescribed value calculation automatically applies a 50% discount when vehicle age exceeds 5 years at the payroll period date
The fuel benefit prescribed value (RM1,800/year) is also halved for vehicles meeting the >5 year threshold
The driver benefit (RM7,200/year) does NOT receive the 50% discount — it is computed separately at full prescribed rate
EA Form BIK section reflects the age-discounted taxable value for all qualifying aged vehicles
technicalNotes: LHDN Public Ruling 11/2019, paragraph 10: for a motor car older than 5 years, the employer shall use 50% of the prescribed value
This rule applies to the Prescribed Value Method only; the Formula Method (cost/lifespan × 80%) does not use this discount
Vehicle age is measured from the ORIGINAL registration date, not from when the current employer acquired or leased it
Public Ruling 11/2019 PDF: http://lampiran1.hasil.gov.my/pdf/pdfam/PR_11_2019.pdf
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module
BIK Motor Vehicle Engine Capacity Prescribed Value Table Lookup for EA Form

userStories.title: Implement Year-End Payslip BIK-to-EA Form Reconciliation Check Before EA Form Generation
userStories.description: The annual EA Form must declare the total monetary value of Benefits-in-Kind received by each employee, which should equal the sum of monthly BIK amounts computed on payslips throughout the year. Discrepancies arise when mid-year vehicle changes occur, the valuation method switches between prescribed and formula, or manual overrides are applied on individual months. A pre-EA Form generation reconciliation check is needed to catch and resolve mismatches before EA Forms are distributed or filed.
acceptanceCriteria: Year-end BIK reconciliation report shows per employee: sum of monthly payslip BIK amounts vs EA Form BIK computation, with a variance column
Discrepancies above RM0.01 are flagged with the specific month of divergence and the likely cause (e.g., method switch, vehicle change, manual override)
Report can be run at any point during the year — not only at year-end — for ongoing BIK accuracy monitoring
EA Form generation is blocked (or displays a prominent warning) for any employee with an unresolved BIK reconciliation discrepancy
HR can mark a discrepancy as 'Reviewed and Accepted' with a mandatory reason note, which removes the EA Form generation block for that employee
technicalNotes: BIK monthly amounts are stored as a custom salary component on Salary Slip
Sources of discrepancy: prescribed vs formula method switch mid-year, vehicle replacement, retrospective Salary Slip corrections, or manual amount overrides
LHDN Public Ruling 11/2019 governs BIK valuation methodology: http://lampiran1.hasil.gov.my/pdf/pdfam/PR_11_2019.pdf
This check is an extension of the existing BIK calculation module and EA Form generation feature
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module
Rebuild EA Form to Include All Mandatory LHDN Section A/B/C/D Fields

userStories.title: Mid-Year Tax Residency Status Change Workflow — Switch Between Resident and Non-Resident PCB Rates
userStories.description: An employee who becomes non-resident mid-year (leaves Malaysia, drops below 182 days) or conversely achieves residency mid-year triggers a PCB rate switch. Non-residents pay a flat 30% on all employment income with no personal reliefs. Residents use the progressive rate scale with reliefs. When an employee's residency status changes, all prior-month PCB amounts for the current year must be reviewed and the remaining months must use the new rate. LHDN requires the employer to adjust withholding prospectively from the month of status change. If the employee becomes non-resident, the employer must also withdraw all TP1 relief applications for that year.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee master has a 'Tax Residency Status' field (Resident / Non-Resident / Pending Determination) with effective date
When status is changed to Non-Resident with an effective date, PCB for all subsequent months uses the 30% flat rate; all TP1 reliefs are suspended for that year
A residency change event is logged in the PCB audit trail with the effective date, previous status, new status, and the HR user who made the change

When Non-Resident status is set, the system generates a warning: 'Prior-month PCB for this year should be reviewed — non-resident rate is not retroactively applied by the employer; advise employee to seek LHDN guidance for year-end reconciliation'
Re-setting status back to Resident re-enables TP1 reliefs from the effective date with a warning noting that LHDN approval may be required for the mid-year switch back
technicalNotes: LHDN Tax Treatment for Residents & Non-Residents: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/if4noy3t/tax-treatment-residents-non-residents.pdf
Residency determination is based on physical presence in Malaysia: 182 days rule under S.7 of the Income Tax Act 1967
Special rules exist for day-counting across calendar year boundaries (S.7(1)(b) and S.7(1)(c))
Flat 30% non-resident rate applies to ALL employment income including bonus and BIK — no exemptions for personal relief
PCB Computation Specification 2025: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
dependencies: Implement PCB Change Audit Trail DocType
Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: Section 107D ITA — WHT 2% Deduction, Monthly Remittance, and CP58 Integration for Agent/Dealer Payments
userStories.description: Section 107D of the Income Tax Act 1967 requires payers to deduct 2% withholding tax (WHT) at source from monetary and non-monetary incentive payments to agents, dealers, or distributors exceeding RM5,000 per year. The WHT must be remitted to LHDN by the 15th of the following month and the deducted amount reported on the annual CP58 statement under Section 83A. This is a separate compliance workflow from the existing CP58 statement generation story, requiring payment tracking and remittance scheduling.
acceptanceCriteria: System identifies payments to recipients classified as Agent/Dealer/Distributor and accumulates annual total per recipient
When cumulative payments to a recipient exceed RM5,000 in a calendar year, 2% WHT is computed on each subsequent payment
Monthly WHT remittance schedule is generated per payer company showing total WHT payable and recipient breakdown
WHT amounts are linked to corresponding CP58 records for annual reporting
LHDN payment reference (after WHT remittance) is stored on each remittance record
System tracks non-monetary incentive values (e.g., vehicles, property) at cost incurred by payer for WHT base calculation
technicalNotes: Section 107D ITA 1967 and Section 83A ITA 1967 (CP58 reporting obligation)
WHT rate: 2% of monetary/non-monetary incentive value; threshold: RM5,000/year per recipient
Remittance deadline: 15th of the following month; late payment attracts 10% penalty
CP58 must be issued to the agent/dealer by 31 March of the following year
Different from employment income PCB (Schedule A); targets commission-based non-employment income
dependencies: Implement CP58 Agent/Dealer Non-Employment Income Statement

userStories.title: Gig Workers Act 2025 — SKSPS Per-Transaction Contribution Calculation and PERKESO Monthly Aggregate Remittance
userStories.description: Under the Gig Workers Act 2025, platform providers must deduct SKSPS contributions from each completed gig transaction (ride, delivery, freelance job) and remit the monthly aggregate to PERKESO. Unlike standard SOCSO (monthly salary-based), SKSPS is computed per-transaction at 1.25%, requiring a distinct computation engine that accumulates transaction-level deductions and generates a monthly PERKESO remittance file.
acceptanceCriteria: SKSPS deduction at 1.25% is computed per completed gig transaction value and attributed to the gig worker
Failed, cancelled, or refunded transactions are excluded from the SKSPS contribution base
Monthly aggregate SKSPS amount per gig worker is computed and held for PERKESO remittance
PERKESO monthly remittance file is generated with worker-level breakdown (NRIC/passport, SKSPS number, amount)
Transaction-level SKSPS deductions are reflected in the gig worker payslip with total SKSPS deducted
Monthly remittance is due by the 15th of the following month consistent with SOCSO payment cycle
technicalNotes: SKSPS contribution rate: 1.25% per transaction; full amount borne by platform provider
Contracting entity is responsible for both computing and remitting SKSPS (not split with worker)
Remittance to PERKESO via PERKESO ASSIST portal; file format to be confirmed from PERKESO documentation
Self-Employment Social Security Act 2017 (Act 789) and Gig Workers Act 2025
dependencies: Gig Workers Act 2025 — Auto-Register Gig Workers Under PERKESO SKSPS via ASSIST Portal API

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60A — Enforce 104-Hour Monthly Overtime Cap with Pre-Payroll Violation Alert
userStories.description: Section 60A(4) of the Employment Act 1955 (post-2022 amendment) limits overtime to a maximum of 104 hours per month for covered employees. The payroll system currently calculates OT pay amounts but does not validate whether total OT hours in a month exceed the statutory cap. Exceeding this cap without Director General approval is an offence; a pre-payroll cap check is required.
acceptanceCriteria: Overtime hours entered per employee per month are accumulated and checked against the 104-hour statutory cap
Early warning alert is raised when any covered employee's monthly OT reaches 90 hours (threshold configurable)
Hard cap alert is raised when any covered employee's monthly OT exceeds 104 hours
Payroll finalisation is blocked (or requires documented manual override with justification) when cap is exceeded
Compliance report lists employees by OT hours for the month with cap utilisation percentage
System applies cap check only to employees covered under the Act (monthly wage ≤RM4,000 or manual labourers); managerial staff are excluded
technicalNotes: Employment Act 1955 Section 60A(4): maximum 104 OT hours per month
Covered employees: those earning ≤RM4,000/month, manual labourers regardless of wages, vehicle operators, supervisors of manual labourers
Does NOT apply to managerial/executive employees earning above the EA coverage threshold
Post-2022 amendment also reduced normal working week from 48 to 45 hours
Director General of Labour approval required to lawfully exceed 104-hour cap
dependencies: Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

userStories.title: Generate SOCSO Borang 2 First-Time Employee PERKESO Registration for ASSIST Portal
userStories.description: When an employer hires a new employee who has never been registered with PERKESO before, the employer must complete Borang 2 (Employee Registration Form) within 30 days of the hire date and submit it via the PERKESO ASSIST portal. The existing PRD covers Borang 3 (new employment notification for already-registered employees) and Borang 4 (termination), but not Borang 2 for first-time registrations. Failure to register within 30 days is an offence under the Employees' Social Security Act 1969.
acceptanceCriteria: New Employee record creation triggers a check: if employee has no existing SOCSO/PERKESO number, system flags for Borang 2 registration
Borang 2 PDF is generated with employee's IC/passport, full name, date of birth, address, and employer details pre-filled
30-day registration deadline alert is raised on Day 1, Day 20, and Day 28 if Borang 2 status remains unsubmitted
PERKESO registration number field on Employee record is populated after successful ASSIST portal submission
If employee already has a PERKESO number (prior employer), system routes to Borang 3 (new employment notification) instead
Compliance report lists employees without PERKESO numbers who have been employed for more than 30 days
technicalNotes: PERKESO ASSIST portal: assist.perkeso.gov.my — employer submits Borang 2 online
Employees' Social Security Act 1969: Section 7 requires employer registration of new employees within 30 days
Borang 2 is for first-time PERKESO registration; Borang 3 is for Notification of New Employment (employee already registered)
Foreign workers require a separate Foreign Worker Registration Form (not Borang 2) — distinguish by nationality
Domestic servants have separate registration form as per Social Security Act exemption amendments
dependencies: Implement SOCSO Borang 3 — New Employee Notification to PERKESO

userStories.title: Progressive Wage Policy (PWP) Eligibility Flag and Wage Escalation Compliance Report
userStories.description: Malaysia's Progressive Wage Policy (PWP), launched June 2024 and expanded in 2025 with a RM200M allocation, incentivises MSMEs to raise employee wages through structured escalation. Participating employers must maintain minimum 6% annual salary increases for employees earning RM1,500–RM4,999/month and meet training requirements. As PWP adoption grows (50,000+ workers targeted by 2027), ERP payroll must be able to flag enrolled employees, track wage progression, and generate audit reports for the Ministry of Human Resources.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record has PWP enrolment flag and scheme entry date field
Payroll annual review computes wage increase percentage from prior year base for each PWP employee
Alert raised if a PWP employee's annual wage increment falls below 6% threshold at year-end payroll cycle
PWP eligibility check: employee's monthly salary must be in RM1,500–RM4,999 range at time of enrolment
Annual PWP compliance report shows each enrolled employee's salary at enrolment, current salary, year-over-year percentage increase, and compliance status (met/failed)
Report is exportable in MOHR-compatible format for PWP application/renewal submissions
technicalNotes: PWP administered by Ministry of Human Resources (MOHR); application via jobsmalaysia.gov.my or MOHR portal
PWP is voluntary but employers receive incentives/matching funds for participation
Wage escalation requirement: minimum 6% increase per year for enrolled employees
Eligible employees: Malaysian citizens, monthly wage RM1,500–RM4,999, employed by MSME
Training compliance is also required (eTRIS or HRD Corp training records) — link to HRD Corp story
As of February 2026 report, over 51,000 workers benefit and median entry-level wage rose from RM1,900 to RM2,200
dependencies: Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700 (Feb 2025 Amendment)

userStories.title: Enforce Director Dividend Distribution PCB Exclusion to Prevent Incorrect PCB Deduction
userStories.description: Company directors who are also shareholders may receive dividend distributions from the company in addition to their director fee or salary. Under Section 25 of the Income Tax Act 1967, dividends are investment income (not employment income) and must NOT be subject to PCB deduction by the employer. The payroll system currently handles director fee vs director salary distinction, but lacks a classification guard to prevent payroll administrators from incorrectly routing dividend payments through the Salary Slip module, which would trigger an erroneous PCB deduction.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Dividend Distribution' salary component type is introduced and classified as non-employment income
When a dividend component is included in any Salary Slip, system raises a hard-block validation: 'Dividend distributions are not employment income — process via Journal Entry, not Salary Slip'
EA Form excludes any amount tagged as dividend distribution from Section A employment income totals
Director employee profile shows total dividend distributions for the year separately from salary/fee for reference (not for PCB)
System guide/tooltip explains correct accounting entry for dividend distributions vs director fee/salary
If dividend component is accidentally submitted on a Salary Slip, system logs a compliance warning and requires manual override with reason
technicalNotes: ITA 1967 Section 25: definition of employment income excludes dividends
Dividends are taxable under Schedule 7 (investment income) when received by individual shareholders — not subject to employer PCB withholding
Single-tier dividend system: company pays dividends from after-tax profits; recipient does not need to pay further tax
Director fee (Section 13 employment income) IS subject to PCB; dividend is NOT — payroll must clearly separate these
Distinct from 'Distinguish director fee vs director salary at invoice level' story — this covers dividend exclusion, not fee vs salary
dependencies: Distinguish director fee vs director salary at invoice level

userStories.title: Record FWCMS Monthly Levy Payment Receipt Reference on Foreign Employee Record
userStories.description: After paying the Foreign Worker Levy via the FWCMS portal (fwcms.gov.my), employers receive a payment receipt with a unique reference number per foreign worker per month. The existing PRD covers levy tracking and payment deadline alerts, but does not capture the post-payment receipt reference. MOHR and Immigration Department audits require employers to produce evidence of levy payment for each foreign worker — the receipt reference number is the primary evidence. This story closes the audit trail loop by storing the FWCMS receipt reference on the foreign employee's levy payment record.
acceptanceCriteria: Foreign Worker Levy Payment record has a FWCMS Receipt Reference Number field
After levy payment is made on FWCMS portal, HR records the receipt reference and payment date on the employee's levy record
Monthly levy status per foreign employee shows: expected levy amount, payment date, FWCMS receipt reference, and payment confirmation flag
Annual audit export generates a report of all FWCMS payments per foreign employee with receipt references for Immigration/MOHR inspection
Alert is raised for any foreign employee levy month where payment date has passed but FWCMS receipt reference has not been recorded
Batch import of FWCMS receipt references is available (CSV/Excel upload) for employers with many foreign workers
technicalNotes: FWCMS portal: fwcms.gov.my — managed by Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA)
Foreign Worker Levy payment deadline: 10th of each month (separate from EPF/SOCSO/EIS deadline of 15th)
Receipt reference format from FWCMS portal to be confirmed; typically an alphanumeric transaction ID
Multi-Tier Levy Model (MTLM): levy rate varies by sector, worker nationality, and tier — reference existing levy tracking story for rate calculation
Receipt reference must be retained for at least 7 years per MOHR record-keeping requirements
dependencies: Implement Foreign Worker Levy Tracking (FWCMS / Multi-Tier Levy Model)
FWCMS Foreign Worker Levy: 10th-of-Month Payment Deadline Alert Separate from EPF/SOCSO

userStories.title: Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End
userStories.description: Under Employment Act 1955 S.60A, unused annual leave that is not taken within the leave year must either be carried forward (by agreement) or paid out at the basic rate. The EA 2022 Amendment strengthened employee protections — employers cannot unilaterally forfeit unused leave without the employee's agreement. ERPNext's standard leave module does not enforce Malaysian EA-specific carry-forward rules or the mandatory cash-out offer before forfeiture. Payroll must enforce that expiring leave is either carried forward or paid out, with documented employee consent for any forfeiture.
acceptanceCriteria: Annual Leave (EA) carry-forward is configurable with maximum carry-forward days (default: 1x annual entitlement, e.g., max 22 days)
At leave year-end, system generates 'Leave Expiry Notice' per employee showing: days expiring, cash-out amount at basic daily rate, carry-forward option
HR Manager must record employee's decision (Carry Forward | Cash Out | Mutual Forfeiture Agreement) before closing leave year for that employee
Cash-out of unused annual leave = (Monthly Basic Salary / 26) × leave days, recorded as non-statutory pay component on Salary Slip
System blocks leave year close-off for any employee without a recorded leave expiry decision
Audit log records all leave carry-forward and cash-out decisions with HR manager approval and employee acknowledgement timestamp
technicalNotes: EA S.60A(3): Employer must pay employee for unused annual leave on termination at basic rate
EA S.60A(4): Leave should be utilized within 12 months of entitlement year
Cash-out rate: Basic Rate of Pay per day = Monthly Basic Salary / 26 (or number of working days per month per contract)
EA 2022 Amendment added protections against unilateral forfeiture
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60A

userStories.title: TP1(1/2026): Capture New Domestic Tourism Attraction Expenses Relief (RM1,000) in Employee PCB Declaration
userStories.description: Malaysia Budget 2026 introduced a brand-new individual income tax relief category in the TP1(1/2026) form: expenses for entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes conducted within Malaysia, capped at RM1,000 per year of assessment. This category has never appeared in any prior TP1 form. The PCB engine must add this as a new deductible item when employees declare it on their TP1(1/2026) relief form. While the generic 'Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories' story addresses the budget's general TP1 changes, domestic tourism is an entirely new relief code with no prior-year equivalent requiring discrete field mapping, validation, and PCB computation integration.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration form adds field: `domestic_tourism_expenses` (Currency, label: 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions / art & cultural programmes in Malaysia')
PCB calculation deducts declared `domestic_tourism_expenses` (capped at RM1,000) from chargeable income when computing monthly MTD
Field only available in TP1(1/2026) form — not retroactively applied to prior-year TP1 records
Validation: amount cannot exceed RM1,000; system shows 'Maximum RM1,000 for YA2026 domestic tourism relief' if exceeded
Year-end PCB reconciliation report shows domestic tourism relief declared per employee
This field is absent from TP3(1/2026) — domestic tourism is a current-employer TP1 item, not a prior-employer YTD income item
technicalNotes: From LHDN TP1(1/2026) explanatory notes: new relief for 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes within Malaysia'
Maximum: RM1,000 per year of assessment (YA2026 only — no retroactive application)
Source form: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/gjuhcbc2/bi-tp1-form-2026.pdf
Explanatory notes: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/i1lcd0wn/bi-explanatory-notes-for-from-tp1-2026.pdf
Confirmed also by briohr.com YA2026 update: 'New Reliefs: Domestic Tourism Expenses — Entrance fees to local tourist attractions — Relief cap: RM1,000'

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1: Permanent RM3,000 Childcare Relief for JKM-Registered Transit Centres
userStories.description: Budget 2026 merged the time-bound RM1,000 childcare relief with the existing RM2,000 relief into a permanent RM3,000 combined relief, and expanded eligible premises to include daily care and after-school transit centres registered with the Department of Social Welfare (JKM) for children up to 12 years. The TP1(1/2026) form and PCB computation engine must reflect this permanent change effective YA2026.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1(1/2026) childcare relief field accepts up to RM3,000 (removing the old RM2,000 regular + RM1,000 time-bound split structure)
System does not apply any year-based expiry restriction to the combined RM3,000 amount for YA2026 onward
TP1 help text and eligibility description updated to include JKM-registered after-school transit centres for children aged ≤12
PCB calculation applies the correct RM3,000 ceiling for this relief line item in the MTD engine
Historical TP1 records for YA2025 and prior retain the old RM2,000 ceiling (no retrospective change)
technicalNotes: Update TP1 DocType relief item field: `childcare_relief_amount` max validation from 2000 to 3000
Remove time-bound relief year guard that previously expired the RM1,000 component
Effective: YA2026 per Finance Bill 2025, gazetted December 2025
Reference: LHDN TP1(1/2026) form, PCB 2026 Specification Amendment
dependencies: Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories and PCB Computation Engine for YA2026 Budget Changes

userStories.title: TP3 30-Day Collection Deadline Compliance Alert for Mid-Year New Hires
userStories.description: LHDN regulations require employees with prior-year income to submit the TP3 form to their new employer within 30 days of joining, and the employer must process it before computing the first monthly PCB. Without TP3, PCB is computed as if the employee has no prior income, systematically under-deducting tax and creating employer audit liability. The system must automatically alert HR when a mid-year new hire is approaching or has exceeded the 30-day TP3 collection deadline.
acceptanceCriteria: System generates a task and email notification 14 days after a new hire's join date when no TP3 record exists and join date is after 1 January of the current year
Red warning banner displayed on first Salary Slip generation for any employee joined after January 1 who has no TP3 record on file
HR dashboard shows a list of employees with outstanding TP3 submissions beyond 30 days from their join date
Alert is automatically cleared once a TP3 record is saved and linked to the employee
January joiners (join date = 1 January to 31 January, first full month) are excluded from the alert — they have no prior-employer income in the current year
technicalNotes: Scheduled daily background job: iterate Employee records where date_of_joining > current year Jan 1 AND no TP3 DocType entry linked
Notification sent via Frappe notification system to HR Manager role
LHDN TP3 guidance: employee should submit TP3 within 30 days of joining; employer should process before first payroll
Penalty for employer non-compliance with incorrect PCB: Section 107A ITA 1967 employer liability
dependencies: Implement TP3 Carry-Forward Declaration for New Hires (Prior Employer YTD)

userStories.title: HRD Corp eTRIS Monthly Levy Payment Deadline Alert and Payment Reference Tracking
userStories.description: HRD Corp requires employers to pay the monthly Human Resource Development levy via the eTRIS portal by the 15th of the following month, with late payment incurring a 10% penalty plus RM2/day. While existing stories cover levy calculation and payment file generation, there is no mechanism to track whether the actual eTRIS payment has been made, record the FPX/JomPay payment reference number, and alert payroll admin when the 15th deadline is approaching with an unpaid balance.
acceptanceCriteria: System generates an automated reminder task/notification to HR Admin on the 10th of each month for the prior month's outstanding HRD Corp levy payment
Payroll admin can record eTRIS payment confirmation: payment_date, payment_method (FPX | JomPay), payment_reference_number per monthly period
Compliance dashboard flags any levy period where payment has not been recorded after the 15th of the month as 'Overdue'
Monthly levy payment summary report shows: period, levy amount, payment status (Paid / Unpaid / Overdue), payment date, eTRIS reference number
Escalation notification sent to HR Manager and Finance Manager when levy remains unpaid after the 20th of the month (5-day late buffer)
technicalNotes: Create 'HRD Corp Levy Payment' DocType: period_month, levy_amount, payment_date, payment_method, payment_reference, status (Unpaid/Paid/Overdue)
Scheduled daily job: set status = 'Overdue' where payment_date is null and today > 15th of the following month
Distinct from: 'HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard' (training claim tracking) and 'HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File' (file generation)
HRD Corp late penalty: 10% of outstanding levy + RM2/day; cumulative until paid
eTRIS portal: portal.etris.hrdcorp.gov.my; accepted payment methods: FPX, JomPay
dependencies: HRD Corp Levy Monthly Payment File and HRDF Employer Portal Integration
Implement HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier (Fixed Allowances vs. Variable Pay)

userStories.title: Annual Leave Cash-Out PCB Computation and EA Form Income Classification
userStories.description: When employees cash out accumulated annual leave under Employment Act S.60A, the cash-out payment is additional employment income in the month of disbursement and is fully subject to PCB. The system must aggregate the cash-out amount with the employee's regular monthly salary for PCB computation purposes, and classify the payment correctly on the EA Form as gross employment income, not as a separate allowance or exempted item.
acceptanceCriteria: Annual leave cash-out Salary Component is flagged as PCB-taxable income (not exempt)
PCB for the payment month is computed on the aggregate of regular gross salary plus the leave cash-out amount
Cash-out amount appears on the EA Form under gross employment income (basic salary/wages line in Section A), not in other income categories
Salary Slip labels the cash-out line item clearly and separately from regular salary for employee transparency
Year-end EA Form reconciliation correctly includes the cash-out amount in total employment income per employee
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.60A(3): employer must pay employee for unused leave at basic rate of pay
Cash-out treated as additional salary in the payment month for PCB purposes (not the irregular/bonus PCB formula unless employer specifically elects that method)
Cash-out rate: Monthly Basic Salary ÷ 26 × number of leave days cashed out
Link to 'Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End' story for leave quantum determination
dependencies: Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End
Add bonus/irregular payment classification for PCB differentiation

userStories.title: Implement TP2 Form — Employee Irrevocable BIK/VOLA Election for PCB Inclusion
userStories.description: LHDN allows employees to make an irrevocable election within a tax year to include Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) and the Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA) as part of their monthly remuneration for MTD/PCB purposes by submitting Form TP2 to the employer. Without TP2 handling, BIK and VOLA are excluded from PCB by default, meaning employees who prefer MTD spreading over lump-sum year-end payment have no mechanism to elect this. The employer must honour the election, adjust PCB computation for the remainder of the year, and retain the TP2 for 7 years.
acceptanceCriteria: A TP2 Election child table or linked DocType on the Employee record captures: election date, BIK monthly value, VOLA monthly value, employer approval status, and effective month
Once approved by employer, the BIK + VOLA amounts are added to the employee's gross remuneration base used in PCB calculation for all subsequent payroll runs within the same assessment year
The election is flagged irrevocable — system prevents reversal within the same assessment year under the same employer
BIK/VOLA amounts added via TP2 do NOT appear in gross salary on the payslip or EA Form gross salary field; they only affect the PCB engine input
System generates a TP2 record PDF with employee name, IC number, employer name, election amounts, and approval signature block
TP2 records are flagged with a 7-year retention requirement in the payroll audit log
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB Specification 2025: BIK/VOLA elected via TP2 is added to gross remuneration for MTD calculation only (not for payslip/EA gross salary)
TP2 is not submitted to LHDN — it is an internal employer record required to be retained 7 years under Section 82 ITA
Reference: LHDN Specification for Computerised PCB Calculation 2025 (https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf)
The irrevocability clause means the PCB engine must lock the BIK/VOLA addend for assessment year once set
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

userStories.title: Implement Employment Act S.25A Digital Payslip Mandatory Fields Compliance Validator
userStories.description: Employment Act Section 25A (as amended by Employment (Amendment) Act 2022) requires employers to provide a payslip to every employee at the time of each wage payment. The payslip must contain mandatory fields including: employee full name, IC/passport number, gender, citizenship status, wage period, wage amount, payment date, overtime earnings, deductions itemised, and employer name. The existing payslip generation may not validate all mandatory fields before generation, exposing employers to MOHR inspection penalties. This story adds a mandatory-field compliance validator to payslip generation.
acceptanceCriteria: Before a Salary Slip is submitted, a validator checks that the generated payslip PDF/print includes: employee full name, IC/passport number, wage period (start and end date), gross salary, itemised deductions (EPF, SOCSO, EIS, PCB, each as separate lines), net pay, and payment date
Missing any mandatory field raises a hard validation error citing 'Employment Act S.25A — Payslip mandatory fields'
The payslip print template is updated to include gender and citizenship status fields if currently absent
A payslip compliance audit report lists any historical salary slips missing mandatory fields
The system confirms LHDN statutory deductions (PCB, EPF, SOCSO, EIS) are itemised individually on the payslip — not combined into a single 'deductions' total
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.25A (post-2022 amendment): employer must give payslip at every wage payment
MOHR inspection can impose fines for non-compliant payslips
Mandatory fields per EA S.25A: name, employee ID, gender, citizenship, wage period, amount, date, all payments/deductions with particulars
Reference: Employment Act 1955 (Akta 265) Section 25A (https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265)_0.pdf)
dependencies: Generate Employment Act S.61 Compliant Payslip with All Mandatory Fields

userStories.title: Track Employment Act Section 60D Public Holiday Triple Pay and LHDN PCB Impact
userStories.description: Employment Act Section 60D mandates that employees working on a public holiday receive triple pay (3x ORP for work on a gazetted public holiday). This is already covered partially by the 'Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator' story, but the PCB/MTD treatment of the public holiday premium is not covered: the triple pay falls under Section 13(1)(a) employment income and is PCB-liable, must appear in EA Form, and impacts monthly MTD. Additionally, Section 60D covers the alternative of a replacement leave day — if replacement leave is taken instead of the extra pay, no additional PCB applies.
acceptanceCriteria: Public holiday premium pay (2x ORP top-up for working on a gazetted public holiday) is classified as regular employment income under Section 13(1)(a) ITA and included in the PCB base for that month
If the employer and employee agree to replacement leave instead of double premium, the system records the replacement leave grant and does not add taxable income
The public holiday work pay component is separately labelled in the EA Form under the applicable remuneration category
PCB calculation for months with public holiday work premium correctly inflates the gross remuneration for MTD purposes
System validates against a configurable list of Malaysian gazetted public holidays (federal + state) to correctly identify which days qualify
technicalNotes: EA Section 60D: employee working on paid public holiday entitled to: (a) ordinary rate + (b) day's wages at ORP = total 2x ORP extra pay (on top of basic), or replacement leave
The premium pay component is regular employment income (Section 13(1)(a) ITA) — subject to PCB
Replacement leave option avoids additional taxable income — must be documented
Reference: Employment Act 1955 Section 60D and employment law analysis (https://www.zulrafique.com.my)
dependencies: Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator (Section 60D Employment Act) with Replacement Leave Tracking
Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

userStories.title: MTD Method 3 — PCB Computation for Weekly and Fortnightly Payroll Periods
userStories.description: The LHDN PCB specification defines three calculation methods: Method 1 (standard monthly), Method 2 (YTD recalculation), and Method 3 for employees paid on a non-monthly basis such as weekly or fortnightly cycles. Method 3 annualizes the periodic gross pay, computes annual MTD using the Schedule 1 formula, then converts back to the pay-period amount. Without this method, employers running weekly or fortnightly payroll have no compliant mechanism to determine the correct PCB per pay run, defaulting incorrectly to a monthly annualization that over- or under-deducts.
acceptanceCriteria: Payroll Entry and Payroll Period support pay_period_type (Monthly / Weekly / Fortnightly / Other) and pay_period_days fields

When pay_period_type is not Monthly, PCB engine applies Method 3: annualized_gross = periodic_gross × (365 / pay_period_days); annual MTD computed from annualized amount less reliefs; period MTD = annual_MTD × (pay_period_days / 365)
CP39 submission correctly aggregates multiple weekly/fortnightly runs within the same calendar month into a single monthly CP39 row per employee
EA Form correctly sums all pay-period MTDs for the assessment year totals regardless of pay frequency
Unit test: employee earning RM1,000 per week produces the same annual MTD as an equivalent monthly employee at RM4,333/month within a RM10 rounding tolerance
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB 2025 Specification PDF: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
Method 3 is defined in the LHDN computerised MTD specification under 'Kaedah Pengiraan Untuk Tempoh Gaji Bukan Bulanan'
Monthly annualization: multiply weekly pay × 52 or fortnightly × 26; then divide annual MTD back by 52 or 26 for per-period deduction
CP39 month mapping: all weekly pay runs in e.g., March → single CP39 March row; aggregate MTD and wages
Custom field on Payroll Period doctype: pay_period_type (Select); default 'Monthly' for backward compatibility
dependencies: Implement PCB/MTD monthly calculation validation
Implement MTD Method 2 (Year-to-Date Recalculation Formula)

userStories.title: Retroactive Salary Arrears PCB Back-Period Spread Calculation
userStories.description: When employers award retroactive salary increases (back pay covering prior months within the same tax year), LHDN guidance allows the additional income to be spread across the months it was earned for MTD recalculation, avoiding a PCB spike in the month of payment. Currently the system treats all retroactive pay as additional income in the month paid (using the bonus/irregular pay pathway), which can cause significant over-deduction in a single month and employee hardship. A retroactive pay workflow that re-computes MTD for each affected prior month and generates corrected CP39 amendment rows is needed.
acceptanceCriteria: Salary Slip includes custom_retroactive_amount, custom_retroactive_period_from, and custom_retroactive_period_to fields
When retroactive_amount > 0, system offers two modes: (a) lump-sum in current month (existing behaviour), or (b) back-period spread — the user selects

In back-period spread mode, system recomputes MTD for each affected prior month by adding proportional retroactive pay to that month's gross, recalculates tax, and collects the incremental MTD as retroactive_pcb_recovery in the current month's Salary Slip
CP39 file includes corrected prior-month rows (one per retroactive month) via the LHDN e-PCB Plus amendment submission format
EA Form classifies retroactive pay under the assessment year it was earned (not year paid) when both fall within the same calendar year
technicalNotes: LHDN guidance: retroactive pay covering prior months in same year may be spread across those months for MTD recalculation — reduces current-month PCB spike
Reference: Talenox LHDN PCB guide: https://blog.talenox.com/malaysia-tax-guide-how-do-i-calculate-pcb-mtd-part-2-of-3/
Cross-year retroactive pay (e.g., 2025 arrears paid in 2026) is taxed in year received — this edge case must be documented and handled separately
LHDN e-PCB Plus allows prior month amendment submissions — use this pathway for retroactive month CP39 corrections
This story complements 'e-PCB Plus Prior Month PCB Amendment' by providing the payroll-side calculation that drives what needs to be amended
dependencies: Generate CP39 monthly PCB remittance file
e-PCB Plus Prior Month PCB Amendment — Trigger Corrected CP39 Row on Salary Slip Resubmission

userStories.title: Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA) Prescribed Value Calculation Module for EA Form Part C
userStories.description: LHDN Public Ruling PR 3/2005 (on Value of Living Accommodation) requires employer-provided housing to be declared as taxable employment income on EA Form Part C. The taxable VOLA is calculated using either: (a) Formula Method: Defined Rate × Annual Value of Accommodation, capped at 30% of the employee's gross annual employment income; or (b) actual rent paid by employer. Without this module, employers providing company housing or housing subsidies are unable to correctly populate Part C of the EA Form and underreport taxable BIK to LHDN. This is distinct from the general BIK module (which covers motor vehicles) and the VOLA election via TP2.
acceptanceCriteria: A VOLA configuration section on Employee or its annual payroll record captures: accommodation_type (Employer-Owned / Employer-Rented / Employee-Rented-with-Subsidy), annual_property_value, actual_annual_rent_paid_by_employer, employee_rent_contribution_annual, and days_occupied
For Employer-Owned: VOLA = min(Defined Rate × annual_value × days/365, 30% of annual gross income from employment)
For Employer-Rented: VOLA = actual annual rent paid by employer (no formula cap applies)
For Subsidized: VOLA = employer-paid portion only (actual rent minus employee contribution)
EA Form Part C (accommodation sub-section) is auto-populated with the computed VOLA amount from the above calculation
Unit test: employee gross RM60,000/year; employer-owned property annual value RM24,000; defined rate 30%: formula = 30% × RM24,000 = RM7,200; 30% cap = RM18,000; VOLA = RM7,200 (formula lower)
technicalNotes: LHDN Public Ruling PR 3/2005 'Value of Living Accommodation' — primary authority for VOLA calculation
LHDN BIK Public Ruling 3/2013 (covers VOLA and perquisites): https://phl.hasil.gov.my/pdf/pdfam/PR3_2013.pdf
Defined Rate per LHDN for accommodation: varies by accommodation type and annual value — verify from latest LHDN Public Ruling
VOLA is declared in EA Form Part C (separate from BIK motor vehicle which is Part C motor vehicle sub-section)
This module extends 'Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module' specifically for the living accommodation category
Talenox VOLA guide: https://help.talenox.com/en/articles/8290345-what-is-the-value-of-living-accommodation-vola-malaysia
dependencies: Rebuild EA Form to Include All Mandatory LHDN Section A/B/C/D Fields
Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

userStories.title: MyInvois Phase 4 (January 2026) — Set Mandatory e-Invoice Flag for Companies with RM1M–RM5M Annual Revenue
userStories.description: Effective 1 January 2026, Phase 4 of Malaysia's MyInvois mandate requires all businesses with annual turnover between RM1 million and RM5 million to comply with mandatory e-invoicing, including self-billed salary slip e-invoices where payroll qualifies as a self-billing scenario. The Company DocType needs a revenue band field so the system can conditionally enable mandatory self-billed e-invoice generation for salary slips and alert HR if MyInvois credentials are not configured.
acceptanceCriteria: Company DocType has custom_annual_revenue_band (Select: <RM1M | RM1M-RM5M | RM5M-RM25M | RM25M-RM100M | >RM100M) and computed custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase (Exempt | Phase4 | Phase3 | Phase2 | Phase1)
When custom_annual_revenue_band = 'RM1M-RM5M', the system enables mandatory self-billed e-invoice generation for applicable payroll documents from 2026-01-01
If custom_einvoice_mandatory_phase is not 'Exempt' and MyInvois API credentials (custom_client_id / custom_client_secret) are blank, a warning banner is displayed on Company page
An e-Invoice Readiness Report lists all companies with their mandate phase and compliance status (Configured / Credentials Missing / Exempt / Voluntary)
technicalNotes: Phase 4 effective: 1 January 2026 for RM1M–RM5M turnover; relaxation period ends 30 June 2026
Phase 5 (previously RM500K–RM1M, due July 2026) is cancelled — threshold raised to RM1M (Cabinet decision 6 Dec 2025)
This story extends the existing 'e-Invoice Mandatory Phase Readiness Check' with the specific Phase 4 revenue band and conditional payroll enforcement logic
LHDN e-Invoice Implementation FAQ: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/0xqitc2t/lhdnm-e-invoice-general-faqs.pdf
dependencies: e-Invoice Mandatory Phase Readiness Check for Employer Company Records
MyInvois SDK v2.1 Compliance Validation for Payroll Self-Billed Invoice Submissions

userStories.title: LHDN AIS Employer Upload — Auto-Submit Employee Remuneration Data to MyTax by 25 February Deadline
userStories.description: LHDN's Auto Income Submission (AIS) system requires employers to upload employee remuneration data to the MyTax employer portal by 25 February each year so that employees' ITRF e-Filing forms can be pre-populated automatically. This is distinct from Form E (deadline 31 March) and EA Form distribution (deadline 28 February). Non-submission of AIS data means employees cannot use the LHDN pre-filled e-Filing service and must enter all income data manually. The payroll system should generate the AIS upload file and track submission status.
acceptanceCriteria: A report 'LHDN AIS Employee Remuneration Export' generates the AIS data file for all active employees for the selected tax year, covering: employee income tax number, IC/passport, total gross employment income, EPF deduction, PCB deducted, bonus, allowances, and BIK
The compliance calendar displays AIS submission deadline as 25 February, listed separately from Form E (31 March) and EA Form distribution (28 February)
After submission, HR can mark 'AIS Submitted' with a submission reference number; the system records this and clears the pre-deadline alert
An alert email is sent to HR Admin on 20 February (5-day warning) if AIS submission status is 'Not Submitted' for any company
The export file format matches the LHDN employer data specification for AIS — field order and data types as specified by LHDN MyTax portal guidance
technicalNotes: LHDN AIS portal: accessible via MyTax employer login; employers encouraged to upload by 25 February for employee ITRF pre-fill
AIS data is a superset of EA Form data — reuse the EA Form data extraction logic with additional fields required by AIS format
AIS is separate from e-Data PCB (monthly MTD), Form E/CP8D (annual return), and EA Form (individual employee distribution)
Source: LHDN employer guides at hasil.gov.my/en/employers/ and QuickHR LHDN compliance guide
dependencies: Generate EA Form (Borang EA) for Employees
Generate Borang E (Form E) Employer Annual Return

userStories.title: HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert — Pre-Warning Before Accumulated Levy Is Lost
userStories.description: HRD Corp's policy states that if an employer makes no training claims for 2 years from initial registration or from the date of the last approved claim, the levy accumulated during that 2-year period is forfeited (HRD Corp retains up to RM10,000 from the remaining balance). The system currently alerts on the 15% under-utilisation penalty but does not separately track the 2-year forfeiture deadline. An 18-month inactivity warning allows HR to take action — submit a claim, arrange training, or flag the situation to management — before the forfeiture date.
acceptanceCriteria: LHDN Payroll Compliance Report includes a 'HRD Corp Levy Forfeiture Risk' row showing: last claim date, months since last claim, and projected forfeiture date (24 months after last approved claim or HRD Corp registration date)

A monthly scheduled job creates a High Priority Notification if months_since_last_claim >= 18, alerting HR Manager with the message: 'HRD Corp levy accrued since [date] is at forfeiture risk on [date]. Submit a training claim before this date to avoid loss of levy funds.'
Company HRDF Settings stores hrd_last_claim_date that HR can update manually when a claim is submitted externally
The alert is separate from the 15% under-utilisation alert — both can be active simultaneously
technicalNotes: HRD Corp forfeiture rule: no training claim in 2 years from registration or last financial assistance → levy for those 2 years is forfeited; HRD Corp retains up to RM10,000 from balance
This is distinct from the 15% under-utilisation penalty (which applies to annual unclaimed levy as a percentage of total levy paid)
Source: HRD Corp FAQ at https://hrdcorp.gov.my/faq
Enforce alert threshold at 18 months to give HR a 6-month window before the 24-month forfeiture triggers
dependencies: Track HRD Corp Levy Utilization Rate and Alert on 15% Under-Utilisation Penalty

userStories.title: Budget 2026 TP1 — EV Charging Facility Relief (RM2,500) as a Distinct Line Item Extended to YA2027
userStories.description: The RM2,500 individual tax relief for EV charging facility expenses (installation, rental, hire-purchase, or subscription fees) is extended through YA2027. This is a distinct relief from the general RM2,500 lifestyle relief (which covers computers, phones, CCTV, and food waste disposers). Employees who declare EV charging costs must have a separate TP1 field to prevent double-counting against the lifestyle relief cap. The LHDN TP1(1/2026) form lists EV charging as a separate claimable item.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration DocType has a field custom_ev_charging_relief (Currency, max RM2,500) that is distinct from the custom_lifestyle_relief field
PCB calculation applies EV charging relief as a deductible item independent of the lifestyle relief cap — an employee can claim both RM2,500 lifestyle AND RM2,500 EV charging in the same year
EA Form Part C shows EV charging relief under its correct LHDN section code matching the TP1(1/2026) layout
Unit test: employee claiming full RM2,500 lifestyle + full RM2,500 EV charging = RM5,000 total deductible, applied correctly in MTD calculation
technicalNotes: Budget 2026 extended EV charging facility relief: YA2022–YA2027
Eligible: installation cost, rental fee, hire-purchase equipment cost, subscription fees for EV charging
LHDN TP1(1/2026) specification PDF at https://www.hasil.gov.my — verify exact section placement for EV charging vs lifestyle relief
This is separate from the RM2,500 eco-friendly lifestyle items (CCTV, food waste disposer) added in Budget 2026
dependencies: Implement TP1 Employee Relief Declaration DocType and PCB Integration

userStories.title: EPF Automatic Foreign Worker i-Akaun Registration (October 2025) — Suppress Manual Registration for EP/VP Holders
userStories.description: Effective October 2025, EPF automatically registers non-Malaysian citizen employees who hold a Visitor's Pass (Temporary Employment) or Employment Pass directly upon employer contribution submission — eliminating the need for employees to visit EPF offices or for employers to manually register them via i-Akaun. The payroll system currently prompts HR to manually trigger EPF registration for new foreign hires. This workflow must be updated so that for EP and VP holders hired from October 2025 onwards, the system relies on EPF's auto-registration and only alerts if the employee's pass type is a work permit (which still requires manual registration).
acceptanceCriteria: Employee DocType custom_pass_type field (Select: Employment Pass | Visitor Pass (Temporary Employment) | Work Permit | Malaysia PR | Citizen | Other) is used to differentiate auto-registration eligibility

For employees with custom_pass_type = 'Employment Pass' or 'Visitor Pass (Temporary Employment)' hired from 2025-10-01 onwards, the new employee onboarding checklist shows 'EPF Auto-Registration (via contribution submission)' — no manual registration step required
For employees with custom_pass_type = 'Work Permit', the onboarding checklist retains the 'Register EPF Member — i-Akaun' manual step with a 7-day deadline alert

An informational banner on the foreign worker EPF section states: 'EP and VP holders (from Oct 2025): EPF membership is auto-registered when first contribution is submitted. Verify employee details on i-Akaun (Employer) after the first contribution month.'
Unit test: new EP employee hired Nov 2025 does not generate a manual EPF registration ToDo; Work Permit employee does
technicalNotes: KWSP announcement: 'Automatic Registration Eases Process For Employers Of Non-Malaysian Citizen Employees' (Oct 2025) — https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/w/news/automatic-registration-eases-process-for-employers-of-non-malaysian-citizen-employees
Auto-registration applies to Employment Pass and Visitor's Pass (Temporary Employment) holders; Work Permit holders still require manual employer-initiated registration
Employer must still verify the EPF member number appears correctly on i-Akaun (Employer) after the first contribution submission
This story is distinct from the existing '30-Day EPF i-Akaun Registration Deadline Alert for Newly Eligible Foreign Workers' which covers the October 2025 mandatory contribution requirement
dependencies: Enforce 30-Day EPF i-Akaun Registration Deadline Alert for Newly Eligible Foreign Workers (Oct 2025)

userStories.title: SOCSO Foreign Worker Invalidity Scheme — Enforce 0.5% Employee Deduction on Payslip from July 2024
userStories.description: Effective 1 July 2024, foreign workers who are under 55 when first contributing to PERKESO must be enrolled in the Invalidity Scheme in addition to the Employment Injury Scheme. This adds a 0.5% employee-share deduction from the foreign worker's salary (employer also pays 0.5% invalidity on top of the existing 1.25% employment injury). The payroll system must enforce this additional employee deduction for eligible foreign workers and reflect the new combined contribution rate (employer 1.75%, employee 0.5%) for all PERKESO contributions on the SOCSO summary report.
acceptanceCriteria: For employees where custom_is_foreign_worker = True and age_at_first_perkeso_contribution < 55, the SOCSO deduction engine applies the 0.5% Invalidity Scheme employee deduction in addition to the Employment Injury Scheme

Payslip shows two SOCSO deduction rows for eligible foreign workers: 'SOCSO Employment Injury (Employer)' (1.25% — employer borne) and 'SOCSO Invalidity Scheme (Employee)' (0.5% — deducted from salary)
The SOCSO contribution report separates Employment Injury and Invalidity Scheme contribution totals with effective date 2024-07-01 as the cutoff for foreign worker invalidity enrollment

Employees who were 55 or older at first PERKESO enrollment are flagged as 'Invalidity Scheme Not Applicable (age ≥55 at enrollment)' and only the Employment Injury rate (1.25% employer, 0% employee) is applied
Unit test: foreign worker enrolled at age 40 has both Employment Injury and Invalidity deductions; foreign worker enrolled at age 56 has only Employment Injury deduction
technicalNotes: PERKESO gazette: Employees' Social Security (Amendment of First Schedule) Order 2024 — effective 1 July 2024
New total SOCSO rates for foreign workers (age <55 at enrollment): employer 1.75% (1.25% EI + 0.5% IS), employee 0.5% (IS only)
Foreign workers age ≥55 at first enrollment: employer 1.25% (EI only), employee 0% — they are in the Employment Injury Scheme but not Invalidity
Payment to PERKESO: via the combined SOCSO+EIS txt file on ASSIST portal — the Invalidity Scheme contribution appears as a separate row type in the file
dependencies: SOCSO Foreign Worker Invalidity Scheme Inclusion Flag and Rate Update (July 2024)

userStories.title: HRD Corp SBL-KHAS — Direct Payment to Training Provider and Levy Utilisation Ledger
userStories.description: Under HRD Corp's SBL-Khas (Special Training Assistance Scheme), HRD Corp pays the approved training provider directly from the employer's accumulated levy — the employer does not pay upfront. The payroll system tracks levy contributions but does not record approved SBL-Khas grants, resulting in an inflated 'available levy balance' display that does not reflect committed training expenditures. A training grant ledger is needed to show the real available balance: total levy paid minus claimed or committed grants.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'HRD Corp Training Grant' DocType records: grant_type (SBL / SBL-Khas / Industrial), approval_date, training_start_date, training_end_date, training_provider, approved_amount, payment_status (Pending HRD Payment / Paid / Rejected)
HRDF Levy Settings displays a computed field custom_available_levy_balance = total_levy_paid_ytd - sum(approved_training_grants_ytd_unpaid)
When payment_status = 'Paid', the approved_amount is deducted from the total available balance; when 'Rejected', the amount is reinstated
A report 'HRD Corp Levy Utilisation Ledger' shows month-by-month: levy contributed, grants approved, grants paid, grants rejected, and running available balance
An alert fires if custom_available_levy_balance falls below RM500, warning HR that the levy balance may be insufficient for pending training claims
technicalNotes: SBL-Khas: HRD Corp pays training provider directly — no reimbursement to employer; employer's levy balance is debited when HRD Corp issues payment
HRD Corp levy rates: 1% of monthly salary for employers with 10+ employees; 0.5% voluntary for 5–9 employee SMEs
Grant submission via eTRiS portal at hrdcorp.gov.my — claims must be submitted within 6 months of training completion
Forfeiture rule: no claim for 2 years = levy forfeited (up to RM10,000 retained by HRD Corp) — related to the 'HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert' story
dependencies: HRDF Levy Contribution Base Classifier
HRD Corp 2-Year Unclaimed Levy Forfeiture Alert

userStories.title: Employee Share Option (ESOS) Year-of-Award PCB Assessment and Year-of-Exercise Payslip Annotation
userStories.description: LHDN treats gains from employee share option schemes (ESOS) as employment perquisites under ITA S.4(b), taxable in the year of award — not the year of exercise. This creates a mismatch: the employee exercises the option and realises cash in year N, but the tax was assessable in year N-X (when the option was awarded). The payroll system must capture the award date, award price, and market value at award for each ESOS grant, compute the perquisite value for the year of award, and include this in the EA Form for the correct year. The exercise event generates only a payslip annotation with no additional PCB impact.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Share Option Grant' DocType records: employee, grant_date, number_of_options, option_price, market_value_at_grant, vesting_schedule, exercise_date (nullable), exercise_market_value (nullable)

At grant date, the system computes perquisite_value = (market_value_at_grant - option_price) × number_of_options and creates a draft Salary Component entry for inclusion in the EA Form for the grant year
The EA Form for the award year includes the ESOS perquisite value under the correct income section (perquisites) even if the employee has not yet exercised the option

When an exercise event is recorded, the Salary Slip for that month shows an informational annotation: 'ESOS exercised: [N] shares at RM[X]; PCB assessed in year of award [YYYY] — no additional PCB this month'
A report 'ESOS Grant Register' lists all active grants per employee with: grant date, award year, perquisite value assessed, exercise status, and EA Form inclusion status
technicalNotes: ITA S.4(b) perquisite treatment: ESOS gain = lower of (market value on exercise date - option price) or (market value on first exercisable date - option price); assessable in year of award
Source: Azmi & Associates legal guide at azmilaw.com on ESOS tax treatment in Malaysia
LHDN Public Ruling No. 11/2012 (Employee Share Scheme) — verify if superseded by a more recent ruling for post-2024 grants
This story handles the PCB/EA Form implications only; the corporate tax deduction for ESOS costs is a separate topic outside payroll scope
dependencies: ESOS/Share Option Gain Calculation

userStories.title: e-PCB Plus Transition — Archive and Decommission Legacy e-Data PCB and eCP39 File Submission Workflows
userStories.description: The LHDN e-PCB Plus system (fully operational from 3 February 2025) replaces the legacy e-PCB, e-Data PCB, and eCP39 portals. Employers who continue to use legacy file formats or submission paths will encounter upload rejections. The payroll system must decommission legacy PCB submission options, migrate any historic submission records to the new e-PCB Plus status tracking model, and display a clear migration guide for HR teams still using the old workflows. Legacy submission records must be retained for audit purposes but clearly labelled as historical.
acceptanceCriteria: Legacy PCB submission options ('Submit via e-Data PCB', 'Submit via eCP39') are hidden from the UI and replaced by a single 'Submit via e-PCB Plus (MyTax)' workflow with a link to the MyTax portal
Any existing submission records created under the legacy e-Data PCB or eCP39 workflow are migrated to a 'Legacy (Pre-Feb 2025)' status category and retained in the audit history

A migration banner is displayed on the LHDN Payroll module home page until dismissed by HR Admin: 'e-PCB Plus is now mandatory from February 2025. Legacy e-Data PCB / eCP39 submissions are no longer accepted by LHDN.'
The Statutory Compliance Calendar replaces 'e-Data PCB submission' and 'eCP39 submission' events with a single 'e-PCB Plus Monthly Submission (due 15th)' recurring event
Unit test: attempting to create a new submission record with status_type = 'e-Data PCB' raises a validation error redirecting the user to the e-PCB Plus workflow
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus launch: 24 September 2024 (pilot); mandatory from 3 February 2025 at MyTax portal
LHDN directive: all employers using e-PCB, e-Data PCB, and eCP39 must migrate to e-PCB Plus by 15 January 2025 for December 2024 submissions
Legacy portals are being phased out — employers uploading to the old portals after the cutoff receive error: 'Service discontinued'
Source: The Rakyat Post (8 Jan 2025), Pandahrms e-PCB Plus guide, and LHDN official announcement at hasil.gov.my
dependencies: Generate CP39 CSV with Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns
e-PCB Plus Successful Submission — Store Acknowledgement Reference

userStories.title: CP8D Annual Pre-Submission Employee Data Completeness Validator
userStories.description: LHDN requires employers to upload CP8D (Annual Remuneration Return) via MyTax by 25 February to enable automatic pre-population of employees' individual tax forms, with final Form E submission due by 31 March (or 30 April for e-filers). An incomplete CP8D — missing employee TIN, income type breakdown, PCB deducted, EPF/SOCSO/EIS amounts, or BIK values — causes LHDN's auto-population to fail and creates reconciliation failures that trigger systematic cross-checks and audit flags. The existing 'Pre-Submission e-PCB Plus Employee TIN and Tax Category Completeness Validation' story covers monthly e-PCB Plus but does not address the annual CP8D/Form E scope, which requires a broader field set including BIK, allowances, and income classification.
acceptanceCriteria: A CP8D Completeness Check tool (accessible from the Payroll module) scans all employees who received salary in the relevant calendar year and flags those missing any of: TIN (custom_employee_tin), employment_type, total_gross_salary (aggregated from Salary Slips), pcb_deducted_ytd, epf_deducted_ytd, socso_deducted_ytd, eis_deducted_ytd, hrdf_levy_ytd, bik_total_ytd, tax_exempt_allowances_ytd
The completeness report shows a traffic-light status per employee: Green (all fields present), Amber (non-critical fields missing), Red (blocking fields missing — TIN, income, PCB)
Clicking 'Fix' on any flagged employee navigates to the relevant record for correction
